entry.title: Batch File Pickup and Dynamic ZIP Creation in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Picking Multiple Files and Creating ZIP Files in SAP Cloud Integration — A Complete Guide

Introduction  

While developing integrations in SAP Cloud Integration, file handling is one of the most common tasks encountered, and in modern integration scenarios, organizations deal with a large number of files arriving on the SFTP server that must be processed and sent to another third-party system. So, dealing with a large number of files at once can impact the system. To address this, files can be processed in batches so that additional operations can be performed and the interface runs smoothly.

This blog explains how batch file processing and ZIP file creation can be implemented in SAP Integration Suite to handle large volume of files efficiently.

Requirements:  

  • Pick 10 files from the source SFTP server
  • Combine the files into a ZIP archive and post it to another SFTP server

Condition: When the ZIP file is extracted, the original file names must remain unchanged  

This approach is especially useful for controlled batch processing, scheduled integrations, and cases that require dynamic file compression.  

 

Prerequisites — Server and Folder Setup  

Before getting started with the integration flow, ensure that the dedicated SFTP server is configured and the directories are created as shown below. 

Create the following directories on the server:  

  • inbound — Source directory where incoming files are placed   
  • tempstore — Temporary directory used to store appended batch data   
  • outbound — Target directory where final ZIP files are delivered

 

Step-by-Step Implementation  

Step 1 — Start by configuring the SFTP sender adapter to control the number of files processed during each run.  

Key Configuration  

Max Messages Per Poll = 10  (in this scenario) 

This ensures that only 10 files are picked in a single batch.  

 

 

Important Tip  

Using a scheduler with a controlled time interval is recommended.

The batch condition logic might not function as intended if the polling interval is set too frequently (for instance, every 10 seconds). Files may end up being processed individually rather than collectively.

Longer scheduling intervals guarantee that all necessary files are available before processing starts. 

In this scenario, 10 files are processed per batch. If more than 10 files are involved, a recurring condition (for example, “Every 5 min”) or even the Advanced Scheduler can be used to ensure that files are grouped and processed in consistent batches.

 

Step 2 — Store File Metadata Using Content Modifier  

After picking the files, the next step is to preserve both the filename and file content. This is necessary because once files are aggregated, their original metadata may be lost.  

Payload Structure:  

<payload>  
    <filename>${header.CamelFileName}</filename>  
    <body>  
        ${in.body}  
    </body>  
</payload>

This ensures:  

  • Filename is preserved  
  • File content is preserved  
  • Data can be safely aggregated and split later

 

Step 3 — Append All Files into a Single Storage File  

The Send step is used to write all payloads into a single file on a temporary SFTP location.  

This step uses Append Mode.  

Key Configuration  

 Append Mode = Enabled  

 

 

Step 4 — Detect Batch Completion Using Router  

A Router is used to determine whether all files in the batch have been processed.  

The router checks the following condition:  

${property.CamelBatchComplete} = 'true'

What This Means  

  • The property becomes true only after all files in the batch are processed  
  • Processing continues only when the batch is complete  
  • If not complete, the flow ends 

 

 

Step 5 — Read the Combined Data Using Poll Enrich  

Once the batch is complete, the system must retrieve the aggregated file.  

This is done using a Poll Enrich step.  

 

Note

"Before Poll Enrich, the message contains the last processed file, after using the Replace aggregation algorithm, the message is replaced with the combined batch file retrieved from temporary storage."  

The SFTP adapter should be configured accordingly. 

 

 

Step 6 — Add a Root Tag to the Combined Payload  

After retrieving the combined data, a Content Modifier is used to wrap the entire payload inside a root element.  

Payload Structure  

<data>  
${in.body}  
</data> 

Why is this required? 

  • To process the XML a single root element is required and without this, it may be difficult for the splitter to perform the split operation as the structure becomes invalid. 

 

Step 7 — Split the Combined Data into Individual Payloads  

A General Splitter is used to split the entire payload into individual messages. 

 

Step 8 — Extract Filename and Body Using Content Modifier  

With the help of this Content Modifier step, the file name and the data can be extracted from each payload (individual split message). 

Properties Created  

FileName  
DataBody  

Message Body Configuration  

${property.DataBody}  

This camel expression is used to call the file content into the message body of content modifier. 

 

 

Step 9 — Create ZIP Files Using Gather Step  

The Gather step is used to create ZIP file dynamically.  

Key Configuration  

  • Incoming Format = Any  
  • Aggregation Algorithm = ZIP  
  • File Name = ${property.FileName}  

Purpose  

  • Dynamically compress the files 
  • Original file names are preserved 

 

Step 10 — Send ZIP Files to Target SFTP Server  

This is the final step where the created ZIP file is sent to the receiver system. 

 

Scheduling Consideration (Critical) {Refer Step 1}  

The scheduling of the integration flow must be time-specific instead of running it continuously. 

Recommendation~ 

Set the scheduler in such as:  

  • At a fixed time on daily basis
  • Hourly basis 
  • At a particular event  

Why is this required? 

If the integration flow runs too frequently:

  • Files may be processed individually 
  • Files may not be picked in proper batches 
  • Aggregation logic may fail 

Scheduling ensures batch processing is controlled and efficient. 

Output / Result  

After deploying the integration flow, the system successfully processes files in controlled batches and generates a compressed output file.  

Observed Behavior  

  • 10 files are picked in a batch from the inbound folder 
  • A ZIP file is created using the Gather palette element  
  • ZIP filename: Batch20260425120549.zip  
  • Zip file is posted to receiver system 
  • When ZIP file is unzipped original file names are preserved

 

 Conclusion  

This blog post covered how to pick multiple files from SFTP using batch processing, combine them into a zip file using gather and post to another SFTP server, preserving file content and file names. 

I'd appreciate any insights, improvements or feedback on this approach. 

If you have questions about the configuration steps or want to share your experiences, please feel free to leave a comment below. 

Happy Integrating!!!

entry.title: Automating MPL and Payload Retrieval in SAP Integration Suite for Enhanced Monitoring
entry.content:
Why we build this solution,

The primary objective is to design a centralized and automated Monitoring solution capable of dynamically retrieving MPL Logs, extracting payload attachment and provide a reusable framework for alerting through Mails or other channel for Integration support activities.

If there is a specific requirement to identify the failed interfaces with Status Message, its Payloads with the exact error information across the Multiple packages within in the required Date and Time range. That required navigation through several monitoring. Now we can achieve this without going to the Monitoring page.

Introduction

Monitoring and troubleshooting integration flows in SAP Integration Suite can become complex when dealing with multiple packages and interfaces. Accessing Message processing Logs (MPL) and their Payload attachments is essential for debugging, Auditing, and Reporting.

This Blog demonstrates a fully Automated Approach to:

  • Fetch the MPL logs dynamically
  • Filter logs based on Time range and integration flow Name 
  • Retrieve payload attachments
  • Process and store Payload data

Step 1: Create OAuth2 client Credentials

Navigate to Monitor→ Integration and APIs→ Manage Security→ Security Material 

create a new credential of type OAuth 2 client credentials
Maintain:

  • Client Id
  • Client secret
  • Token Service URL

OAuth2 client credentials to securely connect to the SAP APIs.

Step 2: Implementation in Sap Integration Suite

Design an Integration Flow by using a Timer Trigger and then configure all the necessary content Modifier, this is where we need to set the control filtering Parameters for MPL Logs. 

Step 3: configure Http Receiver (MPL ODATA  API)

Configure HTTP Adapter:

  • Method:  GET
  • URL:
  • https://<tenant>/api/V1/MessageProcessingLogs 
  • Headers: Accept: application/Json

Step 4: Extract Message GUID

Parse the MPL response which we get from the O DATA API Call and extract the Message GUID (MPL_ID) for the further processing.

In the similar way we can configure the HTTP Adapter to call ODATA APIs to retrieve the attachments and its corresponding value. 

During the Implementation we were also able to extract the Failed Interfaces error Information for specific MPL ID and send the error logs for Monitoring.  Can be used for Error handling in the later stage of development.

 

Challenges faced during Implementation

One of the key challenges during the implementation was to handle the dynamic retrieval of payload attachment using SAP Integration OData APIs. Since the attachments, Metadata and the Payloads are retrieved through multiple APIs calls, designing the flow dynamically without hardcoded package name required careful handling of the MPL properties.

Another challenge we came across was managing the Namespace handling and the Splitter configuration while processing the XML response from APIs. Proper XPath handling and dynamically routing logic were essential to ensure the reliable payload extraction. And throughout the Designing and Execution of this particular Integration flow I have gained a deeper Learning and insight into SAP Integration suite Monitoring APIs and Dynamic I flow filtering.

 

Conclusion:

This Approach demonstrate how SAP Integration Suite Monitoring capabilities can be significantly Enhanced using automated MPL and payload retrieving mechanism to build a Robust monitoring solution in SAP Integration Suite, this can be further Enhanced to 

  • Integrate with Monitoring Dashboards
  • Automated Alert Mechanism detection
  • Dynamically retrieves MPL logs
  • Fetches payload Attachments
  • Supports audit and Analytics

This approach significantly improves visibility, automation, reduces downtime, and enhance operational efficiency in enterprise integrations.

entry.title: Exploring a Desktop Companion for SAP Integration Suite Development
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

Over the last few years working with SAP Integration Suite landscapes, I kept noticing the same pattern during day-to-day integration work: a lot of time is spent switching contexts between tenants, reviewing artifacts manually, documenting integrations, troubleshooting monitor messages, and preparing deployments across environments.

Especially in landscapes with DEV, QA, and PROD tenants, even relatively simple tasks can become repetitive and fragmented. Comparing iFlows between systems, checking whether artifacts are aligned, reviewing failed messages, or generating technical documentation often involves multiple browser tabs, monitor screens, exported files, and manual notes.

Because of that, I started building a local desktop application called SAP IS Companion as a personal side project to explore whether some of these repeated workflows could be simplified into a more focused developer workspace.

The goal is not to replace SAP Integration Suite itself, but to provide a companion-style workbench for inspection, comparison, documentation, migration preparation, monitoring, and developer support tasks around Integration Suite development.

Tenant Comparison and Landscape Visibility

One of the most common pain points I encountered was comparing content across tenants.

When working across DEV, QA, and PROD environments, it can become difficult to quickly identify:

  • Missing packages
  • Missing iFlows
  • Version differences
  • Deployment inconsistencies
  • Landscape drift over time

Today, many teams still rely on manual checks or exported artifacts to validate tenant alignment.

The comparison workflow in SAP IS Companion focuses on helping visualize those differences between tenants in a simpler way before deployments or migration activities occur.

iFlow Documentation Support

Another area that frequently consumes time is documentation.

Even when integrations are implemented correctly, maintaining updated technical documentation for iFlows can become difficult during active projects or support activities.

One area I’m experimenting with is generating draft documentation from iFlow metadata and structure to help developers and support teams review integrations faster and reduce some of the manual documentation effort.

The idea is not to replace technical review, but to help create a stronger starting point for documentation and knowledge sharing.

Failed Message Review and Incident Context

Monitor investigations are another workflow that can become repetitive, especially during support periods or production incidents.

When reviewing failed messages, developers often need to manually gather:

  • Error details
  • Exception information
  • Message context
  • Monitor references
  • Payload details
  • Suggested troubleshooting notes

I started experimenting with generating clearer incident-style summaries from monitor information to make troubleshooting discussions and handoffs easier between teams.

Developer Helper Tools

I also wanted to explore smaller utilities that could help with common integration-development tasks.

Some examples include:

  • Generating XSDs from payload samples
  • Draft XPath and JSONPath expressions
  • Router condition helpers
  • Content Modifier snippet generation
  • Mapping starter structures
  • Payload inspection helpers

These are relatively small workflows individually, but they appear frequently during iFlow development and troubleshooting.

Authentication and Security Considerations

One of the most important areas I’m thinking through carefully is authentication and enterprise readiness.

Since the application connects to SAP Integration Suite tenants using tenant API credentials, topics such as:

  • credential storage
  • local encryption
  • API scope restrictions
  • least-privilege access
  • auditability
  • enterprise authentication patterns

become extremely important.

Right now, I’m continuing to evaluate safer and more enterprise-friendly approaches around credential handling and tenant access.

I would especially be interested in hearing how others would expect authentication to work in a desktop companion-style application for Integration Suite.

Questions for the Community

I’d be very interested to hear feedback from SAP Integration Suite developers, consultants, and BASIS/admin teams:

  • Which Integration Suite workflows are currently the most repetitive or frustrating?
  • How are you currently comparing artifacts between tenants?
  • Do your teams maintain iFlow documentation manually today?
  • Would generated failed-message summaries or incident-style documentation be useful?
  • Are there other helper tools you wish existed around message mapping, payload analysis, or monitoring?
  • What authentication or security expectations would you have for a desktop Integration Suite companion application?

Conclusion

Building SAP IS Companion started mainly as an attempt to simplify workflows I personally kept running into while working with Integration Suite projects and tenant landscapes.

What stood out to me most during development was how much integration work can lose time in context switching between monitoring, documentation, comparison, and migration-related tasks.

The project is still evolving, and security and authentication remain some of the most important areas I’m continuing to evaluate carefully.

More than anything, I’m interested in hearing how others in the SAP Integration Suite space are currently handling these challenges and whether there are workflows that could benefit from better tooling or developer-focused utilities.

Disclosure: SAP IS Companion is an independent side project and is not affiliated with or endorsed by SAP.

Additional disclosure: AI tools were used to help refine and rewrite portions of this blog post for clarity and structure.

entry.title: Implementing AVRO Serialization and Schema Registry with Kafka Receiver Adapter in Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Integration (CI) provides a standard Kafka receiver adapter that allows integration flows to publish messages to Kafka topics. However, the adapter currently does not support AVRO serialization or Schema Registry integration natively. This limitation is already acknowledged by SAP and is listed as a long-term improvement item on the SAP Influence portal.

In one of our integration scenarios, we had a requirement to publish messages in Confluent AVRO wire format, with schemas managed centrally in a Schema Registry. Since the standard adapter does not yet support this, we implemented a custom solution using Groovy scripting and external libraries.

This blog post focuses on:

  • Working with Schema Registry
  • Handling AVRO serialization
  • Preparing messages for the Kafka receiver adapter

ℹ️ Basic Kafka adapter configuration and CI fundamentals are not covered here, as they are already well documented in SAP Help and SAP Integration Community.

 

Schema Registry

Kafka treats messages as raw byte arrays and does not enforce any structure on the data. This can easily lead to incompatibilities between producers and consumers.

A Schema Registry solves this problem by:

  • Acting as a central repository for message schemas
  • Assigning a unique schema ID to each schema version
  • Ensures both producers and consumers agree on the message structure

For a Kafka producer using Confluent AVRO format, the message must contain:

  • A schema ID
  • The payload serialized according to the corresponding AVRO schema

Therefore, before publishing a message to Kafka, the producer must know:

  1. The schema ID
  2. The schema definition (schema value)

 

Approaches to Retrieve Schema Information

First step is to get the Schema details for the AVRO conversion. We explored two possible options to retrieve the schema ID and schema value inside Cloud Integration capability of SAP Integration Suite.

Option 1: Calling the Schema Registry API

In this approach, the integration flow makes a HTTP call to the Schema Registry REST API to fetch the latest schema ID and schema value dynamically.

HTTP call to Schema Registry

Steps involved
  • Call the Schema Registry endpoint
  • Extract the schema ID and schema value from the response using a Groovy script
  • Store these values in Exchange Properties for later use
Pros
  • Always retrieves the latest schema
  • No manual updates required
Cons
  • Additional HTTP call for every message
  • Slight performance impact and extra cost

This approach is ideal when schemas change frequently or must always stay in sync.

 

Option 2: Storing Schema in Partner Directory (Chosen Approach)

The second approach is to store the schema value statically in the Partner Directory and retrieve it at runtime using a Groovy script. The Schema ID is externalized as a configurable parameter. We opted for this approach because our schemas do not change often.

Schema stored in Partner Directory

Steps involved
  • Store the schema value as a JSON file as Binary Parameters in Partner Directory
  • Extract the schema value in the integration flow using a Groovy script
  • Set the schema id as an externalized Exchange Property in the integration flow
Pros
  • It avoids repeated outbound HTTP calls
  • Improves performance and reduces runtime overhead
Cons
  • Whenever the schema changes in the Schema Registry, the schema value and schema ID in CI must be updated manually
    
//***Step 1 : Get the schema value from Partner Directory***//

    // Instance of Partner Directory Service
    def service = ITApiFactory.getApi(PartnerDirectoryService.class, null); 
    if (service == null){
        throw new IllegalStateException("Partner Directory Service not found");
    }
    
    // get the schema value from the Partner Directory
    def schemaBinary = service.getParameter(streamName, "SchemaRegistry" , BinaryData.class);
    
    // set the schema value to a property if it exists
    if (schemaBinary != null){
        byte[] schemaBytes = schemaBinary.getData()
        schemaValue = new String(schemaBytes, StandardCharsets.UTF_8)
    } else {
        throw new IllegalStateException("Schema not found for stream $streamName in Partner Directory");   
    }

 

Payload Conversion to AVRO Format

Once the schema ID and schema value are available, the next step is to convert the incoming message payload to AVRO format before sending it to Kafka.

Scenario Overview

  • Incoming payload: JSON
  • Target output: Confluent AVRO binary format

The conversion is done using a Groovy script with external libraries.

Inputs to the Groovy Script

The Groovy script requires:

  • Input JSON payload
  • AVRO schema definition
  • Schema ID

Output of the Groovy Script in Confluent Wire Format

The output binary produced by the script follows the Confluent serialization format:

[Magic Byte][Schema ID][Serialized Data]

  • Byte 0 – Magic byte (always 0 for AVRO)
  • Bytes 1–4 – 4‑byte schema ID
  • Remaining bytes – AVRO‑serialized record data

The Kafka receiver adapter publishes this binary data directly to the topic.

External Libraries Used

To achieve the conversion, the following libraries were added as external resources:

  • Apache Avro
  • Jackson (for JSON parsing)
Since this solution relies on external libraries and the AVRO conversion logic can be reused across multiple integration scenarios, it is recommended to implement it as a reusable Script Collection. This approach helps keep individual integration flows lightweight by avoiding repeated library uploads, and it also simplifies maintenance by centralizing the logic in one place.
//***Step 2 : Convert the JSON input to AVRO***//

    ByteArrayOutputStream output = null;
    try {
        def schema = new Schema.Parser().parse(schemaValue);

        // Convert JSON to Avro bytes
        AvroSchema jacksonAvroSchema = new AvroSchema(schema);
        AvroMapper avroMapper = new AvroMapper();
        output = new ByteArrayOutputStream();
        avroMapper.writer(jacksonAvroSchema).writeValue(output, new ObjectMapper().readTree(payload));
        byte[] avroBytes = output.toByteArray();

        // Build Confluent format: [magic byte][schema ID][data]
        ByteBuffer buffer = ByteBuffer.allocate(1 + 4 + avroBytes.length)
        buffer.put((byte) 0)
        buffer.putInt(Integer.parseInt(schemaId.trim()))
        buffer.put(avroBytes)
        buffer.flip()

        // Set header and body
        message.setHeader("kafka.KEY", "")
        message.setBody(buffer.array());

    } catch (Exception e) {
        throw new RuntimeException("Failed to convert JSON to Avro format: " + e.getMessage(), e);
    } finally {
        // Close output stream to prevent resource leaks
        if (output != null) {
            try {
                output.close();
            } catch (Exception e) {
                messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Warning", "ByteArrayOutputStream cannot be closed. " + e.getMessage());
            }
        }
        // Close reader to prevent resource leaks
        if (payload != null) {
            try {
                payload.close();
            } catch (Exception e) {
                messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Warning", "Input Reader cannot be closed. " + e.getMessage());
            }
        }
    }

 

Alternative Approach

It is also possible to implement the solution using only the Apache Avro libraries, without relying on Jackson. However, in our experience, this approach led to challenges when the AVRO schema contained optional fields defined as unions with null (for example, "type": ["null", "string"]).

If your schema does not include union fields, this approach is a better option.

    // Convert JSON to Avro object    
        def reader = new GenericDatumReader<Object>(schema)    
        Decoder decoder = DecoderFactory.get().jsonDecoder(schema, payload)    
        Object datum = reader.read(null, decoder)
       
    // Serialize Avro object to binary   
        def writer = new GenericDatumWriter<Object>(schema)    
        Encoder encoder = EncoderFactory.get().binaryEncoder(output, null)    
        writer.write(datum, encoder)    
        encoder.flush()    
        byte[] avroBytes = output.toByteArray()

 

Conclusion

Until SAP provides native support for AVRO and Schema Registry in the Kafka adapter, this custom approach offers a way to:

  • Work with Schema Registry
  • Serialize messages into Confluent AVRO format
  • Publish compatible messages to Kafka from SAP Cloud Integration 

Finally, I would like to thank Sunny Kapoor for his valuable insights, and support in shaping this blog post.

entry.title: Powering AI-Ready Enterprises with SAP Integration Suite - SAP Sapphire Madrid 2026
entry.content:
Hi Community,


Looking forward to SAP Sapphire Madrid 2026, where the spotlight is firmly on the future of integration, AI, and real-time enterprise architecture. This year’s sessions highlight how integration is rapidly evolving—from a backend capability into a strategic enabler for AI-driven business outcomes.

From consolidating integration landscapes to securely connecting and governing AI agents, and learning from real-world success stories like AstraZeneca, the agenda in Madrid truly showcases what’s next.

Don’t miss the opportunity to gain valuable insights, explore innovative approaches, and see how integration is becoming the backbone of AI-powered enterprises. 


Discover practical workflows for mapping, test creation, incident triage, and documentation, along with necessary controls for AI tool interacting with enterprise APIs and event streams. Attendees will receive a shortlist of safe pilots and guardrails to maintain auditability.

BTP1493: Enterprise-ready AI integration: Securely connect and govern your AI agents


Agentic AI requires a trusted integration foundation. A phased consolidation approach highlights where to standardize first, what to retain, and how to unify governance across APIs and events using API Management and advanced Event Mesh capabilities. Explore a practical scoring model that can help you prioritize migrations and advance AI readiness.

BTP1334: Why organizations consolidate integration tools to prepare for Al​


AstraZeneca’s Axial greenfield SAP transformation is building a single foundation on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) for integration, user experience, and automation. Hear what it uses across SAP BTP and learn about its API‑led and event‑driven integration patterns using Cloud Integration, advanced event mesh, and Edge Integration Cell.

BTP1494: Modernising enterprise integration with SAP BTP at AstraZeneca​


Don’t miss these sessions at SAP Sapphire Madrid—add them to your agenda and take the next step toward becoming an AI-ready enterprise.

entry.title: Portable Local SAML Assertion Generator for SuccessFactors APIs (No Installations required etc.)
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

Here is another way to gerenerate a signed SAML 2.0 bearer assertion locally without any installation for testing SFAPIs.

What It Is

A portable Windows GUI. Unzip, double-click SAMLAssertionGen.bat, done. Python runtime is bundled so there is nothing to install. Language refinement and code development done with the help of AI.

Same assertion logic as the Java original. Same XML structure, same RSA-SHA256 signature, same www.successfactors.com issuer and audience. I didn't reinvent anything, just made it easier to run.

Fill in the fields. Click Generate. Copy to clipboard.

You may save your own SAMLAssertion.properties file so values load automatically or just fill up the GUI.

Setup

1. Unzip anywhere.
2. Edit SAMLAssertion.properties:
 
  • tokenUrl - SF OAuth token endpoint
  • clientId - SF API key
  • userId - SF API user
  • userName - fallback if userId is empty
  • privateKey — Base64-encoded PKCS#8 RSA key downloaded from SF - Manage OAuth2 Client Applications
  • expireInMinutes — default 10, keep it short
 
3. Run SAMLAssertionGen.bat.
4. Click Generate Assertion, then Copy to Clipboard.

 

Using the Assertion

POST {tokenUrl} Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

company_id={companyId}&client_id={clientId}&grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:saml2-bearer&assertion={your Base64 assertion}

In Postman: Body > raw > paste the form string above. The response returns a Bearer token. Add it as an Authorization header on any subsequent API call.

 

 

 

 

Technical Notes

XML mirrors the Java original Issuer and Audience set to www.successfactors.com, NotBefore backdated 10 minutes for clock skew, enveloped XML DSIG with RSA-SHA256. Libraries: lxml, signxml, cryptography — all bundled.

Security

  • Never commit your properties file. It contains your private key.
  • Treat the assertion like a password. It grants API access until expiry.
  • Keep expiration short. 10 minutes is the right default.

Download and extract the ZIP file:

https://github.com/wes-teros14/PortableSAMLAssertionGen

 

Conclusion

This utility simplifies the process of generating signed SAML assertions for SuccessFactors API testing by removing installation steps and packaging everything into a portable executable setup.

entry.title: Best Practices Pipeline for Cloud Integration: Naming Conventions
entry.content:
Co-authored by Slava Kuzyakin.

If you like to run your integration scenarios on the Pipeline for Cloud Integration, you need to create your scenario-specific integration flows as well as various entries in the Partner Directory. Furthermore, you have some flexibility in configuring and setting up the pipeline(s) and pipeline steps, i.e., the integration packages and generic integration flows that are provided on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. Before you start working with the Pipeline, it's worthwhile to spend some time to define your own naming conventions which allow you to setup your integration scenarios and the pipelines in a common way.

This blog post is intended to help you defining your own naming conventions. They are not binding, they are rather meant as a recommendation. At the end, naming conventions pretty much depend on your own requirements and policies and hence can be applied in different ways. However, the guidelines provided here can be seen as a good starting point and, if necessary, can be extended or modified to your needs.

Partner Directory entries

In the Partner Directory, you have the option to create partner IDs for different purposes:

  • A Configuration Scenario defines how an integration scenario is configured and how a message behaves, e.g., whether or not an inbound conversion is needed, what xpath conditions should be carried out in a content based router pattern, how many retries should be done in case of a message failure before the message is sent to a dead letter queue, etc.
  • A System Alias represents your communication component, whether sender or receiver, within your pipeline. It is independent of your system landscape.
  • A Business Component is a communication component which name is independent of the system landscape, e.g., a communication component representing a file share.
  • A Business System is a communication component which name depends on the system landscape like for scenarios that are connected via XI or IDoc protocol where the system name on the DEV landscape differs from the system name on the PROD landscape. A business system is mapped to a system alias via an alternative partner in the Partner Directory entry of the system alias.
  • For IDocs, we have lately introduced the partner ID type representing a Party Alias. This can be used for partners of type not equal LS where we map the IDoc control record fields that describe the partner to a party alias.

Naming conventions:

  • Use a prefix for your configuration scenario names like CS_
  • We would treat system alias and business components the same, so that you have the option to define a business component as business system at a later point of time with no need to change the configuration scenario if you decide otherwise. So, here you can use the same prefix BC_
  • For the business systems, you have to stick to the names of your existing systems in your landscape, so here you follow your own naming conventions anyway. Otherwise, you may use the prefix BS_
  • For the party alias, you may use the prefix PRTY_

Scenario-specific integration flows

For the scenario-specific integration flows like for inbound processing, inbound conversion, outbound processing, various custom exits, etc., you can actually follow the same naming convention that we apply when generating those integration flows using the migration tool. If you use the migration tool, right now you need to stick to our naming convention anyway. Note, this will change soon, there are plans to make the proposed integration flow names in the migration tool editable.

In the migration tool, the following naming conventions apply in general:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP<pipeline step number>_<pipeline step description>[_<party name>][_<system name>][_IFIndex_<index number>]
  • ProcessDirect endpoint if applicable: /<scenario name>/pip<pipeline step number>[/<party name>][/<system name>][/IFIndex<index number>]

The <scenario name> is the name of your configuration scenario as defined in the Partner Directory.

PIP stands for Process Integration Pipeline. You may omit it and just have the pipeline step number on its own.

The <pipeline step number> corresponds to the sequence of the pipeline steps that a message is processed within the pipeline although not always all pipeline steps are carried out.

The <pipeline step description> shortly describes the pipeline step. You may omit it if you like to shorten the name.

Depending on the pipeline step, you need to add a system and optionally a party if you communicate to a business partner. The <system name> can be either a sender system or a receiver system depending on the context.

The <index number> of the receiver interface applies to the scenario-specific outbound processing flow only. if you have multiple interfaces for the same receiver, the index number is incremented, see below.

Scenario-specific inbound processing

So, for the scenario-specific inbound processing flow, the following applies:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP01_InboundProcessing

In case of a sender wildcard scenario where multiple sender systems reuse the same configuration scenario, the following applies:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP01_InboundProcessing[_<party name>]_<system name>

Example: CS_Scenario_A01_PIP01_InboundProcessing_BC_System_ABC (for a system without party)

Scenario-specific inbound conversion

For the scenario-specific inbound conversion flow, the following applies:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP03_InboundConversion
  • ProcessDirect endpoint: /<scenario name>/pip03

In case of a sender wildcard scenario, each sender may have a different inbound conversion, so we need an own integration flow for each sender system for which an inbound conversion should be carried out:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP03_InboundConversion[_<party name>]_<system name>
  • ProcessDirect endpoint: /<scenario name>/pip03[/<party name>]/<system name>

Example: CS_Scenario_A01_PIP03_InboundConversion_BC_System_ABC with endpoint /CS_Scenario_A01/pip03/BC_System_ABC

Scenario-specific outbound processing

For each receiver and corresponding receiver interface, an own scenario-specific outbound processing flow is generated. If a receiver has multiple receiver interfaces, an interface index number is incremented. We do not use the receiver interface name here because it may not be ambiguous, you may have xpath conditions for determining the interfaces with the same receiver interface name but different mappings for instance.

So, here the following applies:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP07_OutboundProcessing[_<party name>]_<system name>_IFIndex_<index number>
  • ProcessDirect endpoint: /<scenario name>/pip07[/<party name>]/<system name>/IFIndex<index number>

Example: CS_Scenario_A01_PIP07_OutboundConversion_BC_System_XYZ_IFIndex_1 with endpoint /CS_Scenario_A01/pip07/BC_System_XYZ/IFIndex1

Custom receiver determination

In case of extended receiver determination, where the receiver is determined via a message mapping, the message mapping can be reused and a custom receiver determination flow is generated instead of an XSLT.

So, here the following applies:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP04_ReceiverDetermination
  • ProcessDirect endpoint: /<scenario name>/pip04/customXRD

So, actually here the endpoint does not strictly follow the overall rule because a suffix is added indicating that the end point relates to a custom receiver determination.

Creating integration flows from templates

When you manually create the scenario-specific flows, you can apply the same or similar naming conventions like we do in the migration tool. You do not need to create the integration flows from scratch, we actually strongly recommend to copy the provided template flow from the Cloud Integration Pipeline - Templates package. This saves you a lot of effort and it ensures that the allowed header list and the exception subprocess is already properly defined.

Note: Each template comes with a description briefly describing what the template is good for. If you copy the template to your own integration flow, the description of the template is kept. So, you may need to change the description in the copied integration flow's metadata.

Setting up your pipeline(s)

A couple of versions ago, we have split up the provided integration flows in four packages to be more flexible in setting up your pipeline, see New integration packages for the Pipeline for Cloud Integration. We distinguish the following use cases:

  • You like to setup multiple pipelines to distribute your messages across multiple pipelines or to have dedicated pipelines in a shared tenant environment
  • You like to use a dedicated JMS queue for a particular receiver system
  • For sending IDoc or XI messages to Cloud Integration, you may setup dedicated generic IDoc/XI inbound processing flows for each sender system to be able for instance to define a sender-specific user role or to stop incoming messages for a specific sender system
  • For sending IDoc or XI messages from Cloud Integration to the backend system, you may setup dedicated generic IDoc/XI outbound processing flows acting as reusable channels

To setup your pipeline supporting those uses cases, you have to copy the respective packages from the SAP Business Accelerator Hub (actually from the Discovery area in your tenant) to the Design area of your tenant. This ensures that the copied packages are not cut off from future updates, see Updates for SAP's Integration Packages. When you copy those provided packages, you are told that the package already exists and hence to avoid overwriting the existing packages you must add a suffix to the copied package. This suffix is then also added to the ID of the integration flows within the copied packages.

Additional pipeline

If you setup an additional pipeline, you need to copy the three packages Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound, Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Pipelines and Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Outbound. Once you have copied the packages, you need to configure the pipeline prefix (default PIP) of the generic integration flows. For the async pipeline steps, the pipeline prefix determines the JMS queue names. For the sync pipeline, it determines the ProcessDirect endpoint of the sync pipeline step.

So, best practice is to use the same or at least a similar name for both the package suffix and the pipeline prefix. Although you need to be aware of the following restrictions and hence may have to adapt the names accordingly.

  • The package suffix supports the following characters: [A-Za-z0-9.]
  • The JMS queue supports the following characters: [A-Za-z0-9_]
  • In total, the JMS queue name must not exceed 80 characters

Examples:

  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Pipelines.PIPXL with pipeline prefix PIPXL
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Pipelines.ORG.ABC with pipeline prefix ORG_ABC

Dedicated inbound/outbound channel

To setup a dedicated inbound channel, you need to copy the package Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound, for a dedicated outbound channel it's the package Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Outbound.

As suffix of the copied package, you can use the business system alias. You may also add a keyword like BC indicating that this package relates to a business component. If you like to define the inbound/outbound channel for a specific pipeline other than the default pipeline, then you can also combine the channel naming with the pipeline naming as described above.

For the use case of a receiver-specific queues, in the Pipeline v2 Generic Step06 - Outbound Processing flow, you actually have the option to define both a pipeline JMS queue prefix and a pipeline JMS queue suffix. Prefix, the fixed queue number Q04 and the suffix will be concatenated building the JMS queue name. Here, the suffix would also be the business system alias taking into account the character restrictions as mentioned above.

Examples:

  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound.BC.UVW100 for business component UVW100
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound.PIPXL.BC.XYZ200 for business component XYZ200 on pipeline PIPXL
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Outbound.BC.ABC.105 with pipeline JMS queue suffix ABC_105 (queue name PIPQ04ABC_105)

Hope those guidelines help you as starting point to define your own naming conventions. If you have feedback, let us know, please leave a comment at the bottom of the blog post.

entry.title: Why Software Today Feels Like the Auto Industry Before Tesla
entry.content:
In my role as a Platform Product Manager at SAP, I see every day how the software industry is entering a phase that strongly resembles the moment when the automotive industry shifted from combustion engines to electric and software-defined vehicles. On the surface, we see incremental improvements. In reality, a fundamental platform shift is happening...

....and it is leading us towards something even bigger: The Autonomous Enterprise.

What the “skateboard” platform and software-defined vehicle were for the auto industry is, in many ways, what AI-first, platform-centric architectures are becoming for enterprise software. At SAP, this is shaping our business and platform strategy. From Business AI and Joule to the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) as the backbone of intelligent, end-to-end business processes and, ultimately, more autonomous business operations.

From Feature Software to AI-First Business Platforms

In the automotive industry, the real game changer was not just the battery. It was the completely rethought vehicle platform: centralized computing, over-the-air updates, and the idea that value creation truly starts after the car leaves the factory.

Enterprise software is undergoing a similar transition. We are moving from isolated feature development in individual applications to building platforms where data, processes and AI are tightly integrated. SAP BTP plays a central role as the data, integration and AI layer across core processes such as finance, supply chain and HR. AI is no longer “just another module”, but an integral part of how we design the platform: from embedded Business AI and Joule to AI agents that orchestrate end-to-end business scenarios and automate decisions.

Organizations that simply “bolt on” an AI feature to existing products are effectively doing what some OEMs did when they tried to electrify a combustion chassis: it works at first, but it does not scale and it does not fully exploit the potential of a new platform.

From Software-Defined Vehicles to the Autonomous Enterprise

If electrification and software-defined vehicles were the first big step, autonomous driving is the next logical stage in the automotive transformation. It builds on the same platform foundation - sensors, connectivity, centralized compute - but moves the value proposition from “assisted driver” to “self-driving system”.

The same pattern is now emerging in the enterprise:

  • AI first made individual processes “assisted”, through recommendations, anomaly detection, and copilots embedded in workflows.

  • The next step is autonomous processes that can sense, decide and act across systems: think of an order that triggers fulfillment, adjusts capacity, renegotiates freight and updates the forecast without human intervention. With humans supervising and steering, not micro-managing.

  • The long-term vision is an autonomous enterprise: a company that continuously optimizes itself based on real-time data, policies and goals, orchestrated by AI and agents running on a robust platform.

Just as autonomous driving requires a software-defined vehicle as its base, the autonomous enterprise requires a software-defined business: clean data, consistent process models and a platform that can host intelligent agents and automation at scale.

The Shift in Value Creation – From Hardware to Services, From Apps to Platforms, From Automation to Autonomy

In the automotive world, value creation is shifting from one-time hardware sales to software functions, data-driven services and mobility ecosystems.

We are seeing a similar pattern in enterprise software, with an additional twist:

  • From license-centric thinking to cloud subscriptions and platform consumption.

  • From isolated IT projects to AI-enabled, end-to-end business processes on a unified platform.

  • From task-level automation to goal-driven autonomy, where systems can propose and execute actions in line with business objectives and guardrails.

Here, SAP has a structural advantage: we are deeply embedded in mission-critical business processes around the globe and have access to highly relevant, trusted business data. Exactly the context that enterprise AI and autonomous scenarios need to be meaningful rather than experimental.

Platform Thinking from a Platform Product Manager’s Perspective

As a Platform Product Manager, I work right at this intersection. We no longer look at integration, APIs, events, data and AI services as a loose toolbox, but as a coherent business platform on which customers and partners can build their own innovation....and progressively more autonomous capabilities.

In practice, this means:

  • Designing and Evolving SAP BTP as an open, enterprise-grade foundation for AI and agentic use cases, not a closed island. Agents need a consistent way to access data, call business APIs and trigger workflows across systems.

  • Treating integration and data quality as the backbone : without a clean integration and data layer, AI remains a demo and autonomy remains a slideware vision.

  • Creating a developer and platform experience where APIs, events, AI services, rules and extensibility tell a single story: sensing events, reasoning over data and acting through workflows and business objects.

In other words: if software-defined vehicles needed a “central compute brain”, the autonomous enterprise needs a business platform brain – and that is where the platform strategy becomes truly differentiating.

To make this more tangible, consider the Order‑to‑Cash (O2C) process as the enterprise equivalent of the path from driver assistance to autonomous driving.

A concrete autonomous enterprise scenario - Order‑to‑Cash as the “autonomous driving” lane : In a first stage, AI improves individual steps: classifying incoming orders, predicting demand, suggesting risk scores, or flagging payment issues. This is similar to Level‑1/2 driver assistance in a car: helpful, but the driver (the user) is still in full control.

With agentic AI on/with SAP BTP and Joule, the pattern shifts: AI agents can monitor the entire O2C flow end‑to‑end, reason over real‑time business data and take actions across systems. For example:

  • An Order Reliability Agent continuously scans open sales orders, checks supply, lead times, logistics constraints and predicts which orders are at risk.

  • If a risk is detected, the agent automatically proposes mitigation options like alternative plants, substitute materials, different carriers or adjusted delivery dates, based on company policies and service levels.

  • A Cash Management or Disputes Agent can, in parallel, reconcile incoming payments, detect anomalies and automatically trigger follow‑ups or dispute workflows, reducing manual reconciliation effort dramatically.news.

From a platform perspective, SAP BTP provides the build, integration, data, workflow and AI services that these agents rely on: APIs to access S/4HANA or CX systems, eventing for business changes, AI engines for prediction and workflow/rules for execution. Joule then acts as the orchestrator and interface: business users can ask natural‑language questions (“Which high‑value orders are at risk this week?”) and approve, override or refine the actions that agents proposes.

This is very close to how autonomous driving stacks are built:

  • Sensors and maps in a car correspond to operational and planning data across ERP, CX and supply chain.

  • The driving policy engine corresponds to business rules, guardrails and objectives (service levels, margin, risk).

  • The autonomous driving controller corresponds to AI agents on SAP BTP and Joule, which continuously sense, decide and act across multiple “lanes” of the enterprise.

The endpoint is not a “hands‑off” company, but an autonomous enterprise where a large share of O2C, source‑to‑pay and plan‑to‑produce runs system‑driven. And people focus on exceptions, design and strategy. Just like a driver in an autonomous vehicle, humans stay in charge of the destination and constraints, while the platform handles the repetitive steering in between.

Legacy Thinking vs. AI- and Autonomy-First Architecture

The key leadership question, in my view, is not: “Where can we quickly add some AI?”

Instead, it is: “What would our product and platform architecture look like if we rebuilt it tomorrow, AI- and autonomy-first, from a clean slate...using the business processes and data we already have?”

A few principles I consider critical, for us and for our customers:

  • Architecture before features: Rethink the platform model first. Data, events, policies and agents...then add capabilities on top.

  • Data and model strategy at the core: Without a clear data foundation, governance and feedback loops, AI and autonomy will stay isolated pilot projects.

  • Platform and ecosystem mindset: Openness, APIs and co-innovation with partners and developers are the lever for scale. No single vendor will “own” all agents or all models.

  • AI embedded, not bolted on: Design AI and agents as a natural layer across processes, data and user journeys, with humans in control and clear guardrails.

  • From workflows to policies and goals: Move from hard-coded workflows towards systems that can optimize against business goals within defined policies. Similar to how autonomous driving optimizes route, speed and safety within traffic rules.

A Question for Platform Leaders on the Road to the Autonomous Enterprise

The automotive industry has learned the hard way what happens when a platform shift is underestimated. In enterprise software, we have the opportunity to avoid repeating that mistake and to use the current AI wave as a stepping stone towards the autonomous enterprise.

If you are responsible for platforms or products...at SAP, on the customer side or in the partner ecosystem, ask yourself two simple questions:

  1. Which parts of your platform would you not build the same way again if you had to start tomorrow, AI-first, from scratch?

  2. What would you need to change so that your systems can not only automate tasks, but autonomously optimize outcomes under clear business guardrails?

That is where your real transformation roadmap begins and the journey we will continue at SAP Sapphire this year, where the next chapter of SAP BTP’s evolution, from intelligent platform to true autonomous enterprise backbone, will come into focus.

Curious to hear:
What would you change in your platform today if your goal was not just automation, but a truly autonomous enterprise tomorrow?

SAP Business Technology Platform, Joule Joule Studio #AutonomousEnterprise SAP Sapphire SAP Business AI

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite: A Trusted Foundation for Enterprise-Ready Agentic AI
entry.content:
SAP Integration Suite: A Trusted Foundation for Enterprise-Ready Agentic AI

AI agents are rapidly moving from market momentum to business priority. For enterprises, the opportunity is not just to build smarter agents, but to put them to work across applications, data, and processes in ways that are connected, secure, and ready for enterprise use.

That is where SAP Integration Suite comes in.

SAP Integration Suite is SAP's comprehensive integration offering that helps enterprises turn AI potential into business value by providing the trusted connectivity, built-in governance, and AI-assisted integration capabilities needed to operationalize AI agents across the enterprise.

 

This comes to life across three key areas of benefits: connectivity, governance, and accelerated productivity.

Connect AI agents to the flow of the business

For AI agents to create real business value, not only do they need model access, they need secure access to the systems, processes, and data that drive the business.

SAP Integration Suite helps extend trusted enterprise integration to AI agents across data, processes, and applications, making it easier to move from pilots to production in three important ways:

  • With support for emerging protocols such as MCP, enterprises can build on their existing API management investments by turning enterprise APIs into governed tools for agent use. This helps customers unlock more value from their investments in APIs, while enabling AI agents to interact with business systems in a more secure and scalable way.
  • Thousands of prebuilt, maintained integration assets, accessible through Joule, SAP's AI copilot embedded across SAP applications and technology, help accelerate agent connectivity across SAP and third-party landscapes. This reduces implementation effort, shortens time to value, and makes it easier to bring agent-driven scenarios into production across complex enterprise environments.
  • Event-driven integration and access to real-time data help agents respond to what is happening now, so they can support relevant, responsive, and business-aware execution.

Connecting AI agents is only part of the equation. To scale with confidence, enterprises also need to govern those interactions with the enterprise-grade controls, visibility, and trust.

Govern design-time and runtime with enterprise confidence for AI agents

As AI agents become more embedded in business processes, governance becomes an essential part of enterprise readiness.

Enterprises need clear control over how agents interact with enterprise systems, including what they can access, what actions they can take, and how those interactions are secured, monitored, and governed. SAP Integration Suite provides trusted design-time and runtime governance so businesses can scale agent adoption with greater confidence. This includes:

  • Govern MCP-based agent access with greater control by exposing curated enterprise APIs as governed tools that agents can securely discover and invoke across SAP and third-party systems, extending trusted API governance and policy control into agent interactions.
  • Manage MCP server discovery and access more deliberately by surfacing verified MCP servers in Developer Hub with trusted identity and authentication, helping enterprises reduce risk and give teams a more controlled path to adoption.
  • Enable more secure, cost-aware, and context-rich AI interactions by bringing together native AI usage management and multi-LLM connectivity across SAP and third-party systems.
  • Strengthen operational trust at scale with observability into MCP executions and runtime guardrails such as Traffic Prediction and Anomaly Detection, helping enterprises identify unusual patterns earlier, anticipate traffic more proactively, and maintain stable agent execution.

This helps enterprises move beyond experimentation and empower agent-driven scenarios with the control, transparency, and confidence required for enterprise use. But connectivity and governance are only a fraction of the value. Enterprises also need a faster and more efficient way to build, run, and scale agent-driven integration scenarios by making agentic integration itself more autonomous.

Accelerate agent connectivity with AI-assistance throughout the integration lifecycle

As demand grows for intelligent, connected business processes, speed and efficiency become increasingly important.

Teams need to reduce manual effort, simplify complex integration work, and bring new agent-driven scenarios to life faster. SAP Integration Suite supports this with AI-assisted capabilities across the integration lifecycle, helping enterprises improve productivity while maintaining the reliability, control, and standards required in the enterprise. This includes capabilities that help teams:

  • Speed up integration design with Joule-assisted integration flow generation
  • Support complex B2B scenarios with AI-assisted message transformation
  • Surface API insights faster with conversational analytics
  • Improve operational efficiency with AI-assisted optimization, troubleshooting, and documentation

The result is faster delivery, more efficient handling of complexity, and stronger operational excellence, helping enterprises realize value more quickly from agent-driven integration scenarios.

When connectivity, governance, and AI-assisted acceleration come together, agentic AI becomes far more than a technology promise. It becomes a practical way to connect intelligence to real business operations, with the context, control, and scalability enterprises need.

SAP Integration Suite helps enterprises make that shift by connecting AI agents to the systems, data, and processes they rely on, governing those interactions with enterprise-grade controls, and accelerating integration with AI-assisted capabilities across the lifecycle. For businesses looking to turn AI agents into real operational value, integration is not peripheral to the story. It is the foundation. 

 

Next Steps

Explore the SAP Integration Suite for agentic AI demo to see how trusted connectivity, runtime governance, and accelerated integration enable a real enterprise agent-driven scenario. You can also learn more about how SAP Integration Suite can support agentic AI here.

entry.title: Connecting SAP S/4HANA with SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh via Proxy Configuration
entry.content:
This blog post intends to complement the existing information on the following blog posts:

SAP S/4HANA integration with SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh

SAP S/4HANA and SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) direct integration

 

Whenever a proxy server is configured for SAP S/4HANA outbound traffic, certain configuration changes must be made, while other changes must be requested through a Service Request.

If your customer landscape uses a proxy server for outbound traffic that must be maintained in the SM59 destination, and you receive the following error when activating the AEM channel, this means that your RFC destination of type G does not support TCPS connectivity through a proxy server. In this case, the procedure for routing outbound traffic for your Event channels will be different. One possible solution is to implement a load balancer to route events from SAP S/4HANA, while using the proxy configuration only for the authentication service.

 

 

Requesting a Hyperscaler Load Balancer for Outbound Traffic

The TCPS protocol is required for the AMQP connectivity configured in the Event Channel. So, a solution is to establish this connection directly using a Hyperscaler Load Balancer. 

For that open a Service Request using the template Create Hyperscaler LB for OUTBOUND traffic to an EXTERNAL source(Azure/AWS/GCP).

 

Select the SAP S/4HANA system that needs to be connected with the AEM broker and insert the requested parameters:

Customer ID - the SAP S/4HANA system that needs to be connected.

Select Hyperscaler - the hyperscaler where the selected SAP S/4HANA system is hosted.

Primary or DR Site - if the service is to be performed on the Primary or the DR site.

Source IP Addresses - the IP address of the selected SAP S/4HANA system.

Destination IP Addresses - the Solace broker instance IP address or the host specified with protocol prefix: tcps://<hostname>

Destination Ports - 5671 (port used for AMQP connectivity)

 

Confirm and schedule service.

 

Updating the OAuth profile in SAP S/4HANA

You have created a AEM channel using a service key and the activation fails with the following error:

You have checked the OAuth configuration using the report OA2C_GENERIC_ACCESS, as described in KBA 3444844, and you are receiving the following error:

>> lcl_oa2c_app->EXECUTE_ATR
granttype Client Credentials configured.

HTTP failure, processing failed, invalid state, invalid timeout or others
oa2c_error
Client configuration error or network problems. See kernel traces.

 

Run transaction OA2C_CONFIG:

1. Select the Configuration Name created by your channel.

2. Under tab Administration - Access Settings - Add the values for proxy host and proxy port:

Save the OAuth2 Profile.

 

Run transaction SM59:

1. Select the type G destination created by your channel for the BTP AEM Validation Service.

2. Under Technical Settings - HTTP Proxy Options - Add the values for Proxy Host and Proxy Service:

Save and close.

 

3. Select the type G destination that you created for the Solace broker.

4. Under Technical Settings - Target System Settings - Maintain the same Host and Port used in Destination IP Addresses and Destination Ports requested in the SR Template Create Hyperscaler LB for OUTBOUND traffic to an EXTERNAL source(Azure/AWS/GCP).

 

5. Under Technical Settings - Target System Settings - HTTP Proxy Options - Remove the values for Proxy Host and Service:

Save and close.

 

Go to transaction  SE38 and execute the report OA2C_GENERIC_ACCESS again. Then confirm that you receive the following message:

 

 

Activating the AEM Channel in SAP S/4HANA

Run transaction IWXBE/CONFIG and activate your channel. Once the channel has been activated, perform a connectivity test.

If your channel activation fails, go to transaction SMICM and check the error in the Trace File.

entry.title: SAP Developer News Special Sapphire 2026 Edition
entry.content:
DESCRIPTION

Sapphire Innovation Guide

https://www.sap.com/topics/events/sapphire/innovation-news-guide-2026#introduction

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro

0:10 Joule Studio

2:13 Integration Suite

3:44 ABAP

Transcription

00;00;00;07 - 00;00;06;21
Announcer Guy
This is the SAP Developer News Sapphire 2026 Special Edition.

00;00;06;23 - 00;00;09;26
Thomas Jung
All right. We're here at Sapphire in Orlando.

00;00;10;02 - 00;00;35;28
Thomas Jung
Actually, the keynote is going on right now, so it was one of the quietest times that we could record a news segment. But I wanted to share with you some of the news from the keynote in case you didn't catch it. There's a lot of developer related interesting news. First of all, the announcement of the new edition of Joule Studio, and I'm actually here in front of the Joule Studio Agent Lab, where people are going to be building agents with the new Joule Studio.

00;00;36;01 - 00;01;01;15
Thomas Jung
Rich, I and Josh are here to help out with that. But as described in the keynote, Joule Studio is a new tool for building agents and applications and extensions. But using an AI agent to do that. So it is Agentic development. You basically describe what you want to build and let the AI do a lot of the work.

00;01;01;15 - 00;01;20;07
Thomas Jung
It builds the specifications for you, you approve them, you iterate with the AI, and then it will start generating the code for you as well. But also discussing the keynote is our openness in this environment. We've integrated with other SAP capabilities like BDC.

00;01;20;10 - 00;01;52;09
Thomas Jung
LeanIX and many other SAP capabilities through MCP servers. And what the Joule studio creates is a code based agent approach. So everything that we're creating, you can push to GitHub and then open in a third party tool like Microsoft Visual Studio Code. We were doing that the other day in the agent lab. We created a project in Joule Studio, and then we opened it in VSCode and continued developing it with other coding agents like Claude.

00;01;52;14 - 00;02;12;01
Thomas Jung
And that's just one of the major announcements here at Sapphire. We're going to bring you in the news. Some of the other major announcements, like those around the upcoming release of ABAP for VS code, and an MSC server for ABAP and other developer related announcements later in the news segment, just for

00;02;12;04 - 00;02;18;12
Tushar Pradhan
Hi everyone. Good morning. This is Tushar, part of our integration product management team. And thank you for joining us at Sapphire.

00;02;18;14 - 00;02;41;04
Gayathri Narayana
Hi everyone. My name is Gayathri. I'm also from the Integration Suite product management team. I'm joining you live from SAP Sapphire in the Sunshine State. And we are very excited about two innovations I want to talk about. One is the MCP Gateway and Integration Suite, which enables agent developers to work with a curated set of APIs by cherry picking them and enabling a very governed and secured access.

00;02;41;05 - 00;02;51;28
Gayathri Narayana
The second functionality is Joule and Integration Suite, which enables our integration developers to be able to generate world class integrations, which covers message transformation and B2B scenarios.

00;02;52;00 - 00;03;14;18
Tushar Pradhan
Great great, great innovations. And let me talk about the innovation with the respect to the process orchestration agent that we have released. So this will allow you to find compatible, you know, reuse capabilities, but also able to give you generate Groovy scripts based on your existing PI/POas well as your custom adapters for your Java mapping. But that's not the end of it.

00;03;14;19 - 00;03;33;08
Tushar Pradhan
In Q4, we are also releasing an agent orchestration, which will help you, you know, just get your agent based integration, be able to generate based on that. And last but not least, the AI gateway, which is governed access to your AI, ensuring that your AI management is governed and is orchestrated well. But that's not the end of it.

00;03;33;09 - 00;03;37;29
Tushar Pradhan
Keep listening and keep hearing about Innovation Guide. You'll hear more about it from an integration soon.

00;03;38;07 - 00;03;44;19
Gayathri Narayana
Thank you. And don't forget to check check out the innovation guide which is linked in the description. Thank you so much and have.

00;03;44;21 - 00;03;46;03
Speaker 3
Bye bye.

00;03;46;05 - 00;04;07;15
Rich Heilman
Hey, friends. Rich Heilman here. We need to talk about ABAP in VS code. A major update is hitting us in June. We are officially releasing the ABAP MCP, and you might be wondering what an MCP actually is. It stands for Model Context Protocol. Just think of it as a secure bridge between your AI coding assistant and your SAP back in.

00;04;07;15 - 00;04;31;14
Rich Heilman
Right now, asking an AI to write ABAP It's like asking it to guess in the dark. It relies on generic web data. It has zero clue about your custom tables or your specific data dictionary. The MCP fixes that completely and that's your VSCode AI. Securely read your live about what does it mean for you? It means highly accurate code suggestions that actually compile.

00;04;31;17 - 00;04;49;20
Rich Heilman
The AI finally understands your specific system. You will spend a lot less time fixing weird AI hallucinations and much more time building solid code. We are really looking forward to getting this into your hands in June. I will have plenty of setup walkthroughs ready for you when it drops, but for now, I'll catch you later.

entry.title: ATUL Architecture #1: Why Utilities Need an Intelligent Core on SAP BTP Introduction Utilities are
entry.content:
Introduction

Utilities are entering a decade defined by real‑time operations, distributed energy, and AI‑driven decisioning. Yet most utility architectures were never designed for this level of speed or complexity. The result is a widening gap between what the grid needs and what legacy systems can deliver.

Many challenges come not from SAP itself, but from the surrounding legacy integrations and custom logic that slow down data and processes

As the industry accelerates toward grid modernization, AMI 2.0, and DER integration, A common pattern is becoming clear: architectural constraints around the ERP often limit real‑time operations more than the ERP system itself:

The ERP is no longer the bottleneck—the architecture is.

Even with SAP S/4HANA, utilities may still encounter challenges such as:

  • Delayed response to real-time grid events
  • Data is trapped in silos between the field (OT) and the office (IT).
  • Rigid, high-cost integrations
  • The inability to scale AI beyond pilots

The missing element is an Intelligent Core—a layer that coordinates events, applies business logic, and supports AI‑driven operations.  An Intelligent Core is a real-time orchestration layer that sits between edge systems and ERP, managing events, applying business logic, and enabling AI-driven operations.

1. The Architecture Gap: Complexity Without Coordination

Today’s utility landscape is a tightly coupled mesh of embedded logic, batch processing, and siloed systems. Data moves slowly, intelligence is fragmented, and every change increase complexity.

At its core, this environment is defined by:

  • Embedded Logic: Custom ABAP tightly coupled within ERP
  • Batch Latency: Critical data processed in delayed cycles
  • Siloed Intelligence: AMI, MDM, and grid data isolated from business processes

The result is predictable:

  • Slow response to operational events
  • High cost of change
  • Limited ability to innovate

The challenge is less about individual systems and more about how they coordinate across the landscape.

2. Reimagining the Core: From Record to Orchestration

A modern core is not just a system of record—it is a system of orchestration.

The Intelligent Core acts as the enterprise’s central nervous system, sitting between the edge (meters, grid, customer channels) and the back office (S/4HANA). It performs functions the ERP was never designed to handle:

  • Real-Time Event Orchestration: Reacting to events as they occur
  • Data Normalization: Validating and standardizing inputs before processing
  • Business Rule Execution: Applying logic consistently across systems
  • AI Enablement: Embedding intelligence directly into operational flows

In this model:

  • S/4HANA remains the System of Record
  • SAP BTP becomes the System of Intelligence

3. Why SAP BTP Is the Foundation

The Intelligent Core should never live inside S/4HANA. It requires a platform designed for real-time processing, modular services, and distributed integration.

SAP BTP provides that foundation.

More importantly, it enforces a critical architectural principle:

S/4HANA handles transactions, while BTP handles orchestration, intelligence, and integration.

Figure 1: Intelligent Core Architecture for Utilities

 

This separation enables:

  • Clean Core by Design
  • Event-Driven Agility
  • Modular Services
  • Operational AI
  • Unified Integration Layer

 In this architecture, BTP plays a central role in enabling real‑time orchestration and modular services. In practice, the Intelligent Core leverages SAP Event Mesh for asynchronous event routing, SAP Integration Suite for secure connectivity, and CAP‑based microservices deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. Business rules can be modeled using SAP Business Rules Service, while AI Core orchestrates model execution and inference. Together, these components form a modular, event‑driven stack that scales independently of the ERP.

4.       Conceptual Flow: From Data to Action

The shift in architecture is simple—but powerful:

  • Ingest: Raw events from AMI, grid systems, and customer channels flow into Event Mesh
  • Process: The Intelligent Core standardizes data, applies business rules, and classifies events using AI
  • Execute: S/4HANA receives only clean, validated, business-ready inputs

This changes the game:

  • From raw data → business events
  • From batch → real-time
  • From system-centric → flow-centric

For example, a meter‑read anomaly event published from AMI enters Event Mesh, triggering a CAP service that validates payload structure and enriches it with master data from HANA Cloud. The service then invokes an AI Core endpoint to classify the anomaly type before routing the result to a workflow service that updates the billing process in S/4HANA. Each step is asynchronous, traceable, and governed through BTP’s event and API management layers.

Below diagram shows how AMI Meters and grid systems send event data into SAP BTP’s Intelligent Core for real‑time ingestion, enrichment, AI‑based classification, and workflow orchestration before validated business events reach SAP S/4HANA for transactional processing and analytics.

Figure 2: End‑to‑End Utility Event Flow — AMI → SAP BTP → SAP S/4HANA 

5.  Real-World Impact: Exception Handling Reimagined

The Old Model: Inconsistent AMI data flows into ERP → Billing exceptions increase → Manual teams intervene → Delays and errors compound

The Intelligent Core Model: Events enter the core → Data is normalized and enriched → AI classifies and resolves known patterns → Only true exceptions reach humans

The Result:

  • Higher billing accuracy
  • Reduced operational overhead
  • Faster meter-to-cash cycles

The Intelligent Core integrates seamlessly with SAP Workflow Management and Business Process Automation, allowing utilities to embed exception‑resolution logic directly into operational processes without modifying the ERP.

6.  The Bottom Line

Utilities do not modernize by upgrading ERP alone. They modernize by re-architecting how systems respond, coordinate, and learn.

The shift is clear:

  • From rigid, transaction-centric systems
  • To event-driven, intelligence-driven architecture

SAP BTP becomes a key platform for enabling event‑driven, intelligent operations.

7.  Technical Reference (for practitioners)

  • SAP Event Mesh — Real‑time event routing
  • SAP Integration Suite — Secure connectivity and API management
  • SAP CAP — Modular service development
  • SAP AI Core — Model orchestration and inference
  • SAP Workflow Management — Process automation and exception handling
  • SAP HANA Cloud — Unified data foundation

 

Next in the Series

ATUL Architecture #2 — Event‑Driven Utilities: Designing Real‑Time Flows on SAP BTP.  We’ll walk through:

  • AMI → BTP → S/4HANA event flow
  • Event Mesh patterns
  • Real‑time vs batch
  • A clean, simple event‑flow diagram

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite - External logging to Datadog Using OpenTelemetry
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) with Software version update 8.42.* has introduced support for OpenTelemetry (OTEL) as an external logging destination, allowing you to stream Message Processing Logs (MPL) directly to OTEL-supported observability platfroms like Datadog, Grafana, Splunk etc for centralized observability.

What is OpenTelemetry? OpenTelemetry is a vendor-neutral framework for collecting, processing, and exporting telemetry data—metrics, logs, and traces. It's widely adopted across the industry and supported by most observability platforms, including Datadog. This standardized approach means your integration logs can be correlated with other systems and applications in your Datadog environment.

In this guide, I will walk through the step-by-step configuration needed to send CPI logs to Datadog using the OTEL integration, including prerequisites, BTP setup, and validation of the data flow.

Prerequisites

Before you begin, ensure you have the following:

  • An active Datadog account with access to the organization settings
  • A Datadog API key (available at Organization Settings > API Keys)
  • BTP subaccount with Connectivity and Destination access
  • Acess in BTP subaccount to create/update service instance of Process Integration Runtime with appropriate roles

Step 1: Configure Your Datadog Setup

First, prepare your Datadog environment to receive logs from CPI.

Get Your API Key

Navigate to your Datadog organization settings and create an API key if you don't have one already. Go to Organization Settings and select API Keys. Create a new key and copy it—you'll need this for the BTP destination configuration.



Identify Your Datadog Region and OTEL Endpoint

Depending on where your Datadog organization is hosted, you'll use a different endpoint. For example:

  • EU region: https://otlp.datadoghq.eu/v1/logs
  • US region: https://otlp.datadoghq.com/v1/logs

Confirm your organization's base URL (it appears in your browser when you log in) and append the OTEL log endpoint accordingly.

Step 2: Create the BTP Destination

Now configure the HTTP destination in SAP BTP that will serve as the bridge between CPI and Datadog.

Navigate to Destinations

Log into SAP BTP, open your integration subaccount, and navigate to Connectivity > Destinations.

Create a New Destination

Click "Create Destination" and configure it with the following parameters:

Name: CloudIntegration_MonitoringDataConsumer
Type: HTTP
Authentication: No Authentication
URL: https://otlp.datadoghq.eu/v1/logs

Authentication set to "No Authentication"?  Yes—the Datadog API key will be passed as a header, not as basic auth.

Add Additional Properties

Scroll down and add the following additional properties. These fields tell Datadog how to classify and organize your CPI logs:
