Property Name Value Purpose

OTELServiceNameMessageProcessingLogsIdentifies the service sending logs in Datadog
OTELScopeNameMPLLogical grouping identifier for CPI logs
OTELScopeVersion1.0.0Version of the log schema
URL.headers.dd-api-key<Your Datadog API Key>Authentication header for Datadog ingestion
TargetSystemOTELSpecifies OpenTelemetry as the target system

Important: Replace <Your Datadog API Key> with the actual API key you created earlier. Store this key securely and never commit it to version control.

After entering all properties, click Save.

Step 3: Activate External Logging in CPI

With the destination configured, you now need to activate external logging at the tenant level.

Create a Service Instance

In your BTP subaccount, go to Services > Instances and Subscriptions. Create a new instance of Process Integration Runtime with the following settings:

  • Service: Process Integration Runtime
  • Plan: api
  • Roles: ExternalLoggingActivate, ExternalLoggingActivationRead

Once created, generate a service key and copy the following credentials:

  • Client ID
  • Client Secret
  • Token URL
  • API Host (base URL for your CPI tenant)

Authenticate and Call the Activation API

Use Postman (or curl) to authenticate with the token URL and then call the external logging activation endpoint. Here's the flow:

1. Get an OAuth token:

POST {TokenURL}
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

grant_type=client_credentials
&client_id={ClientID}
&client_secret={ClientSecret}

Copy the access_token from the response.

2. Activate external logging:

POST https://{TenantName}.{host}.hana.ondemand.com/api/v1/activateExternalLogging?defaultLogLevel=INFO
Authorization: Bearer {access_token}

The defaultLogLevel parameter controls which log levels are sent. Options are:

  • NONE—disable external logging
  • INFO—send info, warn, and error logs (recommended)
  • ERROR—send error logs only

A successful response confirms external logging is now active. You should see logs flowing to Datadog within a few seconds.

Verify Activation Status

To confirm external logging is active, query the status endpoint:

GET https://{TenantName}.{host}.hana.ondemand.com/api/v1/ExternalLoggingActivationStatus('{TenantName}')
Authorization: Bearer {access_token}

A successful response will show the activation status as true.

Step 4: Verify Data Flow and View Logs in Datadog

Once external logging is activated, CPI will begin sending Message Processing Logs to Datadog in real-time.

Querying Logs in Datadog

In Datadog, open the Logs section and search for logs with the service you configured in BTP Destination OTELServiceName.

service:MessageProcessingLogs

You should see MPL entries streaming into Datadog logs.

Parsing MPL Content as JSON

CPI sends MPL logs in a structured format, but the log content itself may be nested. To extract individual MPL attributes and create reports or dashboards, you can use Datadog's Pipelines feature:

Option 1: JSON Parser — If your log body is valid JSON, use the JSON parser to flatten the structure and extract fields. 

Option 2: GROK Parser — For more complex log formats, use a GROK parser pattern to extract specific fields.

Note: My Datadog was missing JSON parser, so I created 2 GROK Parsing rule as below after carfully verifing all the log content format ingested by SAP CI for various combination (ApplicationMessageId, SenderId, CustomStatus, LastError, LastErrorModelStepId etc) to parse all MPL logs.


Once parsed, you can facet logs by integration name, message ID, status, and other attributes to build dashboards, set up alerts, and troubleshoot issues faster.

Maintenance and Troubleshooting

Updating the Destination

If you need to modify the destination (e.g., change the API key or region), follow this sequence:

  1. Call the deactivateExternalLogging endpoint
  2. Update the destination in BTP
  3. Call the activateExternalLogging endpoint again

Skipping deactivation can result in not reflect the destinatinn updates.

Known Limitations

When CPI sends logs via OTEL, the timeUnixNano and observedTimeUnixNano fields are currently set to 0 instead of a proper Unix epoch value for custom headers. Datadog doesn't process these malformed timestamps, so log ingestion still works but only for Run Events and not Custom Headers. I believe SAP will address this in a future update.

Useful API Endpoints

For reference, here are other relevant endpoints for managing external logging:

Deactivate external logging:
POST https://{TenantName}.{host}.hana.ondemand.com/api/v1/deactivateExternalLogging

View external logging events:
GET https://{TenantName}.{host}.hana.ondemand.com/api/v1/ExternalLoggingEvents

Check MPL logs (alternative query):
GET https://{TenantName}.{host}.hana.ondemand.com/api/v1/MessageProcessingLogs

For detailed API specifications, refer to the SAP API Hub documentation on External Logging.

entry.title: ATUL Architecture #2 — Event-Driven Utilities: Designing Real-Time Flows on SAP BTP
entry.content:
ATUL Architecture #2 — Event-Driven Utilities: Designing Real-Time Flows on SAP BTP

Introduction

Most of the utilities organization did not build batch-driven systems because they were wrong. They built them because, for many years, batch processing was enough and serving the business need to generate the mass customer bills and satisfying other business processes like payment processing, return and refunds etc.

Meter reads come daily. Billing jobs ran overnight. Outage analysis could wait a few minutes. But now with the new AI Era this world is changing fast.

That’s means today, utilities operate in an environment where:

  • Smart meters generate continuous streams of events.
  • EV charging creates unpredictable load spikes,
  • Distributed energy resources change grid behavior dynamically,
  • Customers expect instant updates and near real-time visibility,

In this environment, delays are no longer just technical limitations — they directly impact operations and customer experience.

Let’s understand this with a simple example occurs daily.

A customer loses power at 2:05 PM, the smart meter detects the outage immediately.

But the utility system processes that information at 2:30 PM, the question is why and the answer is because the event is sitting inside a scheduled batch cycle. Now that 25-minute delay creates:

  • Slower outage response.
  • Increased call center traffic.
  • Poor customer experience.
  • Potential regulatory exposure.

This is where event-driven architecture becomes critical. And this is where SAP BTP can play a major role in modern utility architecture.

This is exactly where event-driven architecture comes in—and where SAP BTP starts to make a real difference.

1.       Why Event-Driven Utilities Matter (In Real Life)

Utilities are no longer operating predictable, centralized grids.

The grid is becoming:

  • Distributed
  • Dynamic
  • High-volume
  • Event-heavy

Millions of devices are continuously generating signals:

  • Smart meters
  • Grid sensors
  • DER systems
  • EV charging stations
  • Field devices

Traditional batch systems struggle in this environment — not because the technology is bad, but because the operating model has changed.

Event-driven architecture changes the model completely.

That’s means Instead of waiting for scheduled processing windows or batch, systems react when events occur. In simple terms real time processing windows.

That enables:

  • Faster outage detection
  • Near real-time billing updates
  • Automated operational workflows
  • Immediate exception handling
  • Better grid visibility

This shift is not only about speed. It is about building utilities that can respond continuously instead of periodically.

2.       Where SAP BTP Fits In (Simple View)

Think of SAP BTP Event Mesh as the traffic controller for events. In many utilities, integration landscapes become tightly coupled over time.

One system talks directly to another.  Then another connection gets added. Then another.

Eventually, even small changes become difficult and risky.

This is where SAP Event Mesh on SAP BTP becomes important.

Think of Event Mesh as the event backbone of the enterprise.

Instead of systems communicating directly:

  • Systems publish events
  • Interested systems subscribe to them
  • Processing becomes decoupled and scalable

In a utility environment:

  • AMI systems publish meter events
  • Grid applications publish operational events
  • SAP S/4HANA consumes only business-relevant events
  • Other BTP services enrich, filter, or analyze the data

This creates flexibility that traditional point-to-point integration cannot provide.

But there is an important architectural reality here:

Poor event design creates the same problems as poor API design.

If event topics, queue structures, and filtering rules are not designed carefully, Event Mesh can quickly become another bottleneck.

3.       A Simple End-to-End Example (AMI → BTP → S/4HANA)

Let’s walk through a simple but real-time business case or scenario.

Scenario of Power outage detected

  • Step 1 — Meter detects outage: A smart meter immediately detects loss of power.
  • Smart meter sends an outage event immediately.
  • This goes to SAP BTP (via MQTT or API).
  • At this point, no batch job is waiting to process the information later. The event is available immediately.

 

  • Step 2 — SAP BTP Processes the Event

From there:

  • Integration services validate the payload.
  • Business rules evaluate the event.
  • Additional context is added.

For example:

  • Is this a single-home outage?
  • Or are multiple meters reporting outages from the same feeder?

This is where intelligence starts to matter. BTP services can:

  • Correlate events,
  • Filter noise
  • Detect patterns
  • Trigger automation

In many architectures, this becomes the real operational brain of the utility platform.

  • Step 3 — SAP S/4HANA Executes the Business Process

 Once the event is validated:

  • A work order can be created automatically
  • Field crews can be dispatched
  • Customers can receive notifications
  • Device management processes can begin
  • Billing or service workflows can update

S/4HANA remains the system of record. But SAP BTP handles the real-time orchestration layer around it. That separation is important for scalability.

4.        What Actually Changes (Batch vs Real-Time)

Traditional Batch Model

  • Meter data processed hourly or daily
  • Exceptions reviewed manually
  • Outage visibility delayed
  • Billing corrections handled later
  • Integrations tightly coupled

Event-Driven Model

  • Continuous event processing
  • Automated workflow triggering
  • Near real-time outage detection
  • Dynamic operational response
  • Scalable publish/subscribe integrations

But there is an important operational truth many projects have discovered late:

Real-time systems expose process problems that batch systems quietly hide.

When systems react immediately:

  • Data quality issues surface faster, and this is real point of having BTP.
  • Business rule conflicts become visible, immediate effect/result.
  • Operational inefficiencies can no longer stay hidden behind overnight processing.

This is why event-driven transformation is both technical and organizational.

5.       Where Things Usually Go Wrong (From Experience)

Mostly in real projects, challenges show up quickly:

  • Event storms: Millions of AMI events can flood the platform during outages or restoration cycles. Without proper throttling and filtering, downstream systems become overwhelmed.
  • Poor event design: If event topics are designed inconsistently:
    • Consumers become tightly coupled
    • Governance becomes difficult
    • Reusability disappears
  • Overloading S/4HANA: Not every event belongs inside ERP. One of the biggest mistakes is forwarding raw operational noise directly into S/4HANA. ERP should receive business-relevant outcomes — not every technical signal.
  • Duplicate processing: In distributed event systems, duplicate events are normal. Architectures must be designed with idempotency in mind from the beginning.

And the biggest one:

Business rules have become more complex than expected. In most implementations, BTP becomes the brain, not just the pipe.

6.        Architecture Principle That Matters Most

The biggest mindset about this modern architecture shift is this:

SAP BTP should not behave like traditional middleware.

And it should be important to understand since most of the client leadership want new system but same like old. It should behave like:

  • An orchestration platform
  • An event intelligence layer
  • A real-time decision platform

That is a very different architectural role.

7.       Key Patterns That Actually Work

From a practitioner point of view, these patterns matter:

  • Use publish/subscribe, not point-to-point integrations
  • Filter and enrich events before they reach S/4HANA
  • Keep S/4HANA focused on business transactions, not raw events
  • Design for idempotency (avoid duplicates)
  • Design events for reuse and scalability
  • Treat Event Mesh as a platform capability, not just middleware

8.       Final Thought

Important things we need to understand as architects and as organization leaders, moving to event-driven architecture is not simply a technology modernization effort. It will change how utilities operate, most of the way business processes execute may change or enhance. That’s result an organization becoming:

  • Faster decisions which will improve business process.
  • Better customer experience.
  • More automation, less manual work.
  • Less dependency on batch cycles.

But it also requires discipline in design. Because one system reacts instantly:


Its means There is very little room for poorly designed processes, noisy integrations, or unclear ownership.

In real-time utilities, architecture quality becomes operational quality.

Next in the Series

ATUL Architecture #3

In the next article, we move closer to the operational grid layer:

  • OT/IT convergence
  • MQTT ingestion patterns
  • IEC 61850 integration
  • Substation-to-cloud flows
  • Security and isolation models.

This is where utility architecture starts moving from enterprise latency… to grid latency.

entry.title: CPI Process Colorizer and Where used global search - Chrome extensions with the help of Vibe coding
entry.content:
When I started working on all of this, I was after two things:

  1. Make my life easier when refactoring complex flows with no proper documentation (Yes, that's happens).
  2. To see how far the unhinged genius of vibe coding can take me, considering how deeply AI started to integrate with daily routine. (Spoiler alert: pretty far. "Away" or not - it's discussible 😁)

Because once you see flow with 30+ subprocesses with names "Local integration Process 1 .. N", you inevitably start asking the Universe some questions. For example about:

  and  

 

 

Let's start with "Where used" global search as appetizer.

 

CPI Where Used rmbc

Link to Chrome Web Storage. Link to source code at Codeberg.

The idea is to find all mentions of selected text within all flows. It might greatly help, for example: when you are shifting from one Cloud connector to another and you need to change all Location Ids, urls, credentials and etc. and you have no idea which flow has it.

Select text on a page at your CPI tenant (both CF and Neo), right-click, choose "CPI Where Used rmbc" (rmbc = right mouse button click) and the extension opens a new tab with all iFlows where that exact text appears (checking credentials, pgp keys, urls, jms queus, script names and etc. including search in default values and externalized configured params.).

Why contextMenus ? Simply because I have enough buttons from different extensions.

Spoiler


How it works:
The extension:
1. Uses the current CPI tenant URL and existing browser session cookies.
2. Fetches a CSRF token.
3. Loads all CPI packages.
4. Loads artifacts for each package.
5. Filters artifacts to "IFlow".
6. Downloads each "iFlow detail JSON".
7. Recursively searches all string, number and boolean values for the selected text.
8. Opens a result tab showing package, iFlow, JSON path and snippet.

Around 200 flows took a little more than a minute to retrieve and parse.

Since I wanted to avoid redownload everything in case of need of new search within small period of time - caching is introduced.

Caching:
Package/artifact metadata and iFlow detail JSON are cached per tenant for 15 minutes inside the extension service worker. It is not persisted in chrome.storage. So repeating a search shortly after the previous one should avoid much of the repeated downloading and waiting, unless Chrome stops the service worker or the cache expires.

Limitations:
It's obviously can't find dynamically built queue names, since those generated names do not exist within the flow metadata.

It does not unzip full exported packages. As a result it's not parsing inside groovy scripts and etc., so Extension will not find function "ABC" if it's only not mentioned in attributes.
Hm... Idea...

 

And now the "main dish":

 

CPI Colorizer and Process Panel

Link to Chrome Web Storage. Link to source code at Codeberg.

This one visually colorizes SAP CPI processes and subprocess call boxes and shows a searchable process panel from the BPMN model. And most important thing: it navigate you in the model via pan to selected process or its' step. You don't need frantically search in which subprocess, under which label desired script exist.

How it works:
Two editor-only enhancements:

1. Process colorization
The extension reads the BPMN model of the currently opened iFlow and builds a process color matrix. Main Integration Processes, Local Integration Processes, and local process-call boxes are colored consistently. A call box receives the same color as the subprocess it calls, making process relationships easier to understand visually.

Spoiler

2. Searchable process panel
The extension injects an “iFlow Processes” panel into the CPI left navigation area. The panel lists processes, subprocesses, exception subprocesses, and steps in flow order. It includes search, expand all, collapse all and BPMN reload controls. Searching can find step names, process names, scripts, mapping names, tooltips, and configured step attributes.

Spoiler
   

Panel rows are clickable. Clicking a process or step pans/selects the corresponding object in the CPI model and highlights it with a temporary pulse outline.
8Mb gif under the spoiler.

Spoiler

 Honorable mention: gif was created by "Capture to a Gif" extension. Link to Chrome Web Storage

The extension includes a browser popup where users can configure:
- whether Color/Panel is enabled by default
- process/subprocess positioning
- step positioning

Spoiler

It's needed cause everyone is working under different screen resolution and has their own preferences. You can always toogle colorization and panel injection by floating button.

 

Now I will go full nerd mode with the code comments (functions with more than 10 lines would be under the spoiler)... You can skip it to PS: if you want.

What's going on at the editor page:

Side panel:
Custom Panel is injected as the last <li> item in a list of <ul id="shell--navigationList" ...>:

<ul id="shell--navigationList" ...>
  ...
  <li id="cpi-colorizer-side-panel" data-cpi-colorizer="process-panel">
    <!-- extension panel content -->
  </li>
</ul>

It was done intentionally, so it participates in the same navigation scrolling area as the native menu entries. The panel content itself does not use its own max-height or internal scrollbar.

Tried to inject it as separate block with fixed height and separate scroll bar, but with small screen resolution it would be very hard to use and scroll bars would be everywhere, since we would "eat" the space of standard Menu. So moved to "<ul id="shell--navigationList" ...>".

But in this case a bug was encountered: if expand/collapse standard menu injection is flickering. Reason is that SAPUI5 rerenders the list and temporarily removes injected <li>, later the watcher inserts it again.

setInterval(() => {
  ensureSidePanelInjected();
}, 1500);

To make it smooth, it was done via MutationObserver (provides the ability to watch for changes being made to the DOM tree) .

const host =
  document.getElementById("shell--sideNavigationMenu-Flexible-Content")
  || document.getElementById("shell--navigationList")?.parentElement;
// then
sidePanelObserver.observe(host, {
  childList: true,
  subtree: true
});

Checking if <div id="shell--sideNavigationMenu-Flexible-Content"> is available. If not - fallback to <ul id="shell--navigationList">.
When SAPUI5 expands/collapses menu items or rerenders the left navigation, the observer calls

function scheduleSidePanelEnsure() {
  if (sidePanelEnsureRaf) return;

  sidePanelEnsureRaf = requestAnimationFrame(() => {
    sidePanelEnsureRaf = null;
    ensureSidePanel();
  });
}

Executing function ensureSidePanel()

Spoiler
function ensureSidePanel() {
  const navigationList = document.getElementById("shell--navigationList");
  if (!navigationList) return false;

  let panel = document.getElementById(PANEL_ID);
  const wasCreated = !panel;
  if (!panel) {
    panel = createSidePanel();
    navigationList.appendChild(panel);
  } else if (panel.parentElement !== navigationList) {
    // SAPUI5 can rerender the left navigation. If preserved panel is still
    // present but no longer inside the current navigation <ul>, move it back.
    navigationList.appendChild(panel);
  }

  if (currentModel) {
    // Do not repaint the whole panel on every observer check.
    // Only ensure the panel exists after SAPUI5 rerenders the left navigation.
    const body = document.getElementById(PANEL_BODY_ID);
    if (wasCreated || !body || body.childElementCount === 0) {
      renderSidePanelTree(getSidePanelSearchValue());
    }
  } else {
    loadSidePanelModel();
  }

  return true;
}

But the biggest time eater was navigation and positioning. 


Navigation and positioning:
First idea was to navigate SVG nodes

[id*="CallActivity_63"]
[data-sap-ui*="CallActivity_63"]
[aria-label*="CallActivity_63"]

Then just scroll into view

element.scrollIntoView({
  block: "center",
  inline: "center"
});

And emulate mouse events

element.dispatchEvent(new MouseEvent("mousedown", { bubbles: true }));
element.dispatchEvent(new MouseEvent("mouseup", { bubbles: true }));
element.dispatchEvent(new MouseEvent("click", { bubbles: true }));

But that fails. Scrolling was not working at all and diagram was hysterically moved by emulating clicks.

The cause is that CPI has its own pan control with buttons

To find them we need in Chrome Dev Tools (F12) in console tab

[...document.querySelectorAll("*")]
  .filter(el => {
    const text = [
      el.getAttribute("title"),
      el.getAttribute("aria-label"),
      el.textContent
    ]
      .filter(Boolean)
      .join(" ")
      .toLowerCase();

    return text.includes("pan view");
  });

Dumping results to the AI helps a lot to solve the main problem: that clicking emulation not only trigger standard movement behavior but also triggering positioning of the bottom property panel, that changing the size of the box where diagram is.

So solution is "pan" first to the specific anchoring values and only after that "select". Starting this flow by

Spoiler
/**  Entry point for panel click navigation to a Process_* or SubProcess_* or step BPMN id. */
function focusRenderedModelElement(bpmnId) {
  const target = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId);
  if (!target) {
    setSidePanelStatus(`Could not find rendered element ${bpmnId}. Try Reload BPMN model.`);
    return;
  }

  // Selection itself makes CPI/Galilei auto-pan the model. That auto-pan fights
  // with our own pan calculation, so navigation now does the opposite order:
  // first use Galilei's pan buttons to move the target close to our desired
  // viewport anchor, then select it. When the element is already visible in a
  // good position, CPI's selection has little or no reason to move the view.
  const runId = ++panelNavigationRunId;
  lastPanelNavigation = { bpmnId, timestamp: Date.now() };
  preAnchorThenSelectModelElement(bpmnId, runId);
}

 Then find the real SVG object like:

Spoiler
CallActivity_63  → BPMNShape_CallActivity_63
SubProcess_16    → BPMNShape_SubProcess_16
Process_1        → BPMNShape_Participant_Process_1
/**
 * Finds the exact rendered BPMNShape_* SVG group for a BPMN model id.
 */
function findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId) {
  const id = String(bpmnId || "");
  if (!id) return null;

  const diagramId = currentModel?.shapeDiagramIdById?.get(id);
  const candidateIds = uniqueStrings([
    diagramId,
    `BPMNShape_${id}`,
    normalizeProcessId(id) ? `BPMNShape_Participant_${id}` : ""
  ]);

  for (const candidateId of candidateIds) {
    const element = document.getElementById(candidateId);
    if (element && !isExtensionElement(element)) return element;
  }

  // Fallback only. Some CPI builds expose the id in sap-automation or other attributes.
  // Restrict to Galilei/SVG nodes so our own side-panel rows are never returned.
  const escapedId = cssEscape(id);
  return document.querySelector(
    `svg .sapGalileiSymbolNode[id$="${escapedId}"], ` +
    `svg g[sap-automation*="${escapedId}"], ` +
    `svg g[id*="${escapedId}"]`
  );
}

Key function is preAnchorThenSelectModelElement() that is moving view in steps with tick of 22 ms

Spoiler
/**
 * Pans the target into place first, then pulses and selects it in CPI.
 */
function preAnchorThenSelectModelElement(bpmnId, runId) {
  // Run enough pan passes before selecting so a single panel click can cover
  // far-away elements. Earlier versions only did a small number of pan steps,
  // so users had to click the same panel row repeatedly. Galilei pan buttons
  // update the SVG asynchronously, therefore each pass does one small pan and
  // waits briefly before measuring again. The short delay keeps long-distance
  // jumps responsive while still letting Galilei apply each pan step.
  let pass = 0;
  let stableNearAnchorTicks = 0;
  let lastPositionSignature = "";
  const maxPasses = 120;

  const tick = () => {
    if (runId !== panelNavigationRunId || lastPanelNavigation.bpmnId !== bpmnId) return;
    const target = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId);
    if (!target) return;

    const signature = getRenderedElementAnchorSignature(bpmnId);
    if (signature && signature === lastPositionSignature) {
      stableNearAnchorTicks += isRenderedModelElementNearAnchor(bpmnId) ? 1 : 0;
    } else {
      stableNearAnchorTicks = isRenderedModelElementNearAnchor(bpmnId) ? 1 : 0;
      lastPositionSignature = signature;
    }

    if (stableNearAnchorTicks < 2 && pass < maxPasses) {
      anchorRenderedModelElementWithGalileiPan(bpmnId, { pulse: false, maxIterations: 1 });
      pass += 1;
      setTimeout(tick, 22);
      return;
    }

    const finalTarget = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId) || target;
    pulseRenderedModelElement(finalTarget);
    dispatchSyntheticEditorClick(finalTarget);

    // Re-pulse after CPI opens the standard properties area. Do not pan after
    // selection: CPI may auto-adjust the viewport while selecting the object,
    // and a second extension pan would fight that internal movement.
    setTimeout(() => {
      if (runId !== panelNavigationRunId || lastPanelNavigation.bpmnId !== bpmnId) return;
      const selectedTarget = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId);
      if (selectedTarget) pulseRenderedModelElement(selectedTarget);
    }, 450);
  };

  tick();
}

Then just check if we are close enough to selection event.

Spoiler
function anchorRenderedModelElementWithGalileiPan(bpmnId, options = {}) {
  const target = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId);
  if (!(target instanceof Element)) return;

  const viewport = findGalileiViewport(target);
  const controls = findGalileiPanControls();
  if (!viewport || !controls.left || !controls.right || !controls.up || !controls.down) return;

  const maxIterations = Number.isFinite(options.maxIterations) ? options.maxIterations : 12;

  for (let i = 0; i < maxIterations; i += 1) {
    const latestTarget = findRenderedModelElement(bpmnId) || target;
    const targetRect = latestTarget.getBoundingClientRect?.();
    const viewportRect = viewport.getBoundingClientRect?.();

    const anchor = getNavigationAnchorFractions(bpmnId);
    const desiredLeft = viewportRect.left + viewportRect.width * anchor.x;
    const desiredTop = viewportRect.top + viewportRect.height * anchor.y;

    const dx = targetRect.left - desiredLeft;
    const dy = targetRect.top - desiredTop;

    const needsX = Math.abs(dx) > 90;
    const needsY = Math.abs(dy) > 70;
    if (!needsX && !needsY) break;

    if (needsX) {
      clickBestPanButton(
        latestTarget,
        Math.abs(dx),
        dx > 0 ? controls.right : controls.left,
        dx > 0 ? controls.left : controls.right,
        "x",
        anchor
      );
    }

    if (needsY) {
      clickBestPanButton(
        latestTarget,
        Math.abs(dy),
        dy > 0 ? controls.down : controls.up,
        dy > 0 ? controls.up : controls.down,
        "y",
        anchor
      );
    }
  }
}

But it also checks if pan is inverted via clickBestPanButton()

Spoiler
/**
 * Chooses and clicks the most effective Galilei pan button for one axis.
 */
function clickBestPanButton(target, previousDistance, primaryButton, fallbackButton, axis, anchor = { x: 0.45, y: 0.45 }) {
  const beforeRect = target.getBoundingClientRect?.();
  clickGalileiPanButton(primaryButton);
  const afterRect = target.getBoundingClientRect?.();
  if (!isUsableRect(beforeRect) || !isUsableRect(afterRect)) return;

  // If the target did not move or moved clearly in the wrong direction, undo
  // with the opposite control and use that opposite control once. This keeps
  // the function robust if SAP's tooltip wording is interpreted differently.
  const moved = axis === "x" ? afterRect.left - beforeRect.left : afterRect.top - beforeRect.top;
  if (Math.abs(moved) < 0.5) return;

  // The caller passes previousDistance only as a guard against clearly bad
  // moves. Recompute against the viewport anchor for a reliable comparison.
  const viewport = findGalileiViewport(target);
  const viewportRect = viewport?.getBoundingClientRect?.();
  const newRect = target.getBoundingClientRect?.();
  if (!isUsableRect(viewportRect) || !isUsableRect(newRect)) return;
  const desired = axis === "x"
    ? viewportRect.left + viewportRect.width * anchor.x
    : viewportRect.top + viewportRect.height * anchor.y;
  const newDistance = Math.abs((axis === "x" ? newRect.left : newRect.top) - desired);

  if (newDistance > previousDistance + 20 && fallbackButton) {
    clickGalileiPanButton(fallbackButton);
    clickGalileiPanButton(fallbackButton);
  }
}



PS:

It's already passed the point of TL;DR , so pretty short:

Hope those extensions would help at least someone.

Of course, I do not collect any data with it. You can always check source code by provided links and by "%LOCALAPPDATA%\Google\Chrome\User Data\Default\Extensions" path.

AI is obviously unbelievably strong helper but still, unfortunately, not perfect, "Where used" extension was written within two chill hours at the weekend watching football. "Colorizer and Panel" took quite a lot of time because of constant AI attempts to standardize, ignore problems and breaking what was working to solve non-working stuff 😁.  But, in any case, it saves hours and hours of work.


If you have any questions or encountered some bugs (it should be there 🤣) always welcome to write there in the comments, codeberg, email and etc.

entry.title: Modernize SAP PI/PO Landscapes with SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Modernization conversations are happening everywhere right now.  And honestly, many customers are asking the same question: “How do we move from SAP Process Integration / SAP Process Orchestration without disrupting what already works?”

That’s exactly what this upcoming partner webinar is built for.

We’ll walk through the latest migration and assessment tool updates, practical modernization strategies, and SAP’s long-term integration vision.  Plus, you’ll get guidance, resources, and real-world best practices to help customers move confidently to SAP Integration Suite.

The thing is that migration projects are rarely just technical upgrades.  They’re business continuity projects.  The more prepared you are, the easier those customer conversations become.

If you already have a basic understanding of SAP Process Integration / SAP Process Orchestration and SAP Integration Suite, this session will give you practical insights you can apply immediately.

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPIntegrationSuite #DigitalTransformation #EnterpriseIntegration

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite - Operations - Cloud Foundry runtime and service keys
entry.content:
Dear Community,

this blog provides practical insights into structuring Service Keys for SAP Integration Suite, a topic that is often
overlooked during initial setup but becomes critical as landscapes grow.

Intention

With the move to Integration Suite as middleware, customers quickly reach a point where they need to govern access to
their Integrations properly. Who is allowed to trigger which scenario? How are credentials managed, rotated, and
audited? SAP provides many resources to help with the broader landscape setup but the specifics of Service Instance
and Service Key design are rarely addressed in detail.

Approach

We focus on one specific design decision as best practices: how to name and structure Service Instances and Service Keys
for the Process Integration Runtime (pir) in Integration Suite as well as how to protect Integration Flow endpoints using Custom
Sender Roles. Based on multiple customer ramp-ups, a clear and repeatable structure has emerged that scales well and supports both modern and legacy authentication requirements.

Solution

The Problem with unstructured Service Keys:

  • Shared Service Keys used by multiple systems - one key, no traceability
  • Basic Auth credentials shared across scenarios - a single compromised key impacts everything
  • No separation between sender systems or business processes
  • Missing Custom Roles - every key can trigger every iFlow

These patterns make auditing difficult, key rotation risky, and access control nearly impossible to enforce cleanly.

Naming Convention - Service Instances

source: Create a Service Instance in SAP BTP (Dev Tutorial) ,  SAP Help - Service Instances in Cloud Foundry;

Our recommended basic structure for a Service Instance is:

  • pir_{type}_{sendersystem}_{project/scenario}
SegmentDescription Examples
typeRuntime typeflow, api
sendersystemSource system or platform which connects to Integration Suitep01, h01, advancedeventmesh, calm, n8n
project/scenarioBusiness process or project - omit if genericmaterialmaster, payroll, monitoring

Examples: 

pir_flow_p01
pir_flow_p01_materialmaster
pir_flow_advancedeventmesh
pir_api_calm_monitoring
pir_api_integrationsuite
pir_flow_h01_payroll
pir_flow_h01_elster

Important: When a Custom Role is required, create a dedicated Service Instance and Service Key per Custom Role:

  • pir_{type}_{sendersystem}_{project/scenario}_{custom_role}

This ensures that each Custom Role maps exactly to one instance and one dedicated key — no shared access, clean
separation.

Naming Convention - Service Keys

OAuth2 / Basic Auth:
sk_{project/scenario}

Good to know: A Service Key for the process-integration plan provisions both Basic Auth and OAuth2 (Client Credentials) credentials within the same key. This supports backwards compatibility: legacy systems use Basic Auth while modern systems use OAuth2 with the same key, without maintaining parallel credentials.

Examples:

sk_z_report_materialmaster
sk_h01
sk_eventenablement
sk_showcase_salesorder

Certificate-based Authentication - add _c_ after the prefix:

  • sk_c_{project/scenario}

Examples:

sk_c_userexchange_saml2
sk_c_payroll
sk_c_elster

Full Example:

Without Custom Role - one instance, but a dedicated Service Key per scenario to maintain traceability:

Instancepir_flow_p01 
Keysk_p01generic, for simple/shared access e.g. in DEV
Keysk_z_report_materialmasterdedicated, for Z-Report scenario
Keysk_eventenablementdedicated, e.g. for Event Enablement project

Hint: sharing a single key across multiple scenarios defeats the purpose of auditability.

With Custom Role - each Custom Role requires its own dedicated instance and key:

Example 1:

Instancepir_flow_h01_payrollholds this role: PayrollSender
iFlowPayrollSync_H01Integration Flow in Integration Suite
RolePayrollSenderCustom Role assigned to iFlow endpoint
Keysk_c_payrolldedicated key, with certificate

Example 2:

Instancepir_flow_h01_elsterholds this role: ElsterSender
iFlowElsterSubmit_H01_customIntegration Flow in Integration Suite
RoleElsterSenderCustom Role assigned to iFlow endpoint
Keysk_c_elsterdedicated key, with certificate

Hint: A generic key such as sk_h01 would not hold the Custom Role and therefore cannot trigger the protected iFlow - the
dedicated key is mandatory.

When to Create a Dedicated Instance?

SituationDedicated Instance
Different sender systemYes
Sensitive process (Payroll, Elster, HR)Yes
Custom Role requiredYes - one instance and one key per Custom Role
Generic shared flows, no role restriction neededNo - one instance but multiple dedicated keys

Conclusion

This design gives you:

  • Clean audit trails - every call traceable to a specific key and system
  • Safe key rotation - rotating one key affects exactly one scenario or system
  • Controlled access - Custom Role ensures only authorized keys trigger the Integration Flow

A consistent naming convention and the deliberate use of Custom Sender Roles are small investments with significant
operational returns. They give your Integration Suite landscape the auditability and security posture that enterprise
middleware requires - and the dual Basic Auth / OAuth2 support within a single Service Key means you can implement
this without forcing every connected system to upgrade at once.

Feel free to use the comment section or reach out to us via SAP Integration Services.

Modernize. Integrate. Innovate.

entry.title: How to Link Attachments Between SuccessFactors Modules Using SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
In SAP SuccessFactors integrations, there are many business scenarios where attachments need to be transferred between different modules such as Recruiting to Onboarding or Onboarding to Employee Central and other modules. One common requirement is transferring offer letter attachments from the Recruiting module to the Onboarding module during the hiring process.

Although SAP SuccessFactors provides standard OData APIs for attachment handling, implementing this requirement using the SAP SuccessFactors adapter in SAP Integration Suite involves multiple steps. Due to security and module level data separation in SAP SuccessFactors, attachments are not directly shared across modules. Instead, attachments are stored independently and linked using unique attachment IDs.

In this blog post, we will go through the complete step-by-step process of retrieving an offer letter attachment from Recruiting and linking it to the Onboarding module using the SAP SuccessFactors adapter in SAP Integration Suite.

The first step is to retrieve the offer letter attachment details from the Recruiting module in Cloud Integration using SuccessFactors adapter. Connect SuccessFactors adapter with OData V2 and retrieve the offer letter from JobApplication entity as below

Once you have the offer letter retrieved, filter for the latest offer letter if there are multiple letter attached for the same candidate. This can be done using lastModifiedDateTime/offerLetterId/createdDateTime fields to filter

Attachments cannot be directly transferred from one SuccessFactors module to another, for example from Recruiting to Onboarding, due to security and ownership restrictions within SAP SuccessFactors. If you attempt to directly reuse the same attachment ID across modules, you may encounter authorization or access rights errors because the owner and security context of the attachment in Recruiting are different from those in Onboarding.

To overcome this limitation, the attachment must first be retrieved from the source module, then uploaded again to generate a new attachment ID in the target module. Finally, the newly generated attachment ID must be linked to the employee profile in the Onboarding module.

Prepare payload to upload the attachment from Recruiting to general attachment entity in Onboarding 

Use content modifier to prepare the payload like below. Get file content, file name from Recruiting (JobApplication Entity) and store them in property to use them in the payload. The module name in Recruiting is RECRUITING and when we upload into Onboarding, it's GENERIC_OBJECT. UserId is the ID which is used to connect SuccessFactors with Integration Suite.

<Attachment>
<Attachment>
<fileContent>${property.fileContent}</fileContent>
<fileName>${property.fileName}</fileName>
<module>GENERIC_OBJECT</module>
<userId>SFAPI</userId>
<viewable>true</viewable>
</Attachment>
</Attachment>

Upload Attachment to General Attachment Entity 

Connect with SuccessFactors Adapter with request reply and use Upsert (POST) Operation to post the payload prepared in previous step to attachment entity. 

Once attachment is uploaded into generic object, store the attachmentId from the response payload 

Prepare payload to map the attachmentId to respective employee profile in Onboarding

Map the payload to target structure to upload the attachmentId

Note: We do not have to upload the file content, file name again. Instead we can link the attachmentId to employee profile in Onboarding

Upload attachmentId to MDF Object which is created in Onboarding to upload the attachments
Connect with SuccessFactors Adapter with request reply and use Upsert (POST) Operation to link the attachmentId 

Below is the response message after uploading the attachmentId successfully

<?xml version='1.0' encoding='utf-8'?><UpsertResponses><cust_ONB_NewHireDocumentsUpsertResponse><key>cust_ONB_NewHireDocuments/externalCode=100110</key><status>OK</status><editStatus>UPSERTED</editStatus><message null="true"/><index type="Edm.Int32">0</index><httpCode type="Edm.Int32">200</httpCode><inlineResults type="Bag(SFOData.UpsertResult)"/></cust_ONB_NewHireDocumentsUpsertResponse></UpsertResponses>

Reference:
Linking Attachments to an MDF Entity

entry.title: Intelligent Error Handling in SAP CPI Using AI Adapter
entry.content:
Introduction:

SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) provides powerful integration capabilities, but analyzing runtime exceptions and identifying resolutions often requires manual effort. With the new AI Adapter in SAP Integration Suite, we can now build intelligent error handling flows that automatically analyze exceptions using Large Language Models (LLMs) and return meaningful resolutions back to the source system.

In this blog, we will build an AI-powered error handling mechanism where:

  • CPI captures runtime exceptions
  • Sends the error text to an AI model using the AI Adapter
  • AI analyzes the issue
  • Returns Cause and Resolution
  • CPI sends the intelligent response back to the sender

 

Traditionally, when an iFlow fails, support teams manually analyze:

    • MPL logs
    • Exception stack traces
    • Adapter errors
    • HTTP failures

This process can be time-consuming for support teams and integration developers.

Using AI, we can automate:

      • Error classification
      • Root cause analysis
      • Suggested resolution
      • Troubleshooting guidance

This helps reduce support effort and improves response time.

Architecture Overview

Flow:

Sender → Exception Subprocess → AI Adapter → AI Model → Response Back to Sender

The Exception Subprocess captures the CPI runtime error and forwards the error text to an LLM through the AI Adapter.

The AI model then generates:

        • Error Summary
        • Root Cause
        • Resolution Steps

 

Prerequisites

Before starting, ensure the following:

          • SAP Integration Suite tenant
          • AI Adapter available in CPI
          • API Key for OpenAI/OpenRouter
          • AI Receiver Adapter configured

In this example, we use OpenRouter free models for testing purposes.

Step 1: Create Security Material

Navigate to:

Monitor → Security Material

Create:

  • Type: Secure Parameter
  • Name: OPENROUTER_API_KEY

Value:

  • Bearer sk-or-v1-xxxxxxxx

 

Step 2: Configure AI Adapter

Add an AI Receiver Adapter inside the Exception Subprocess.

Adapter Configuration

 

Address

OpenRouter URL/OpenAI URL

Resource Path

/api/v1/chat/completions

HTTP Method

POST

Authentication

API Key Alias- Created in Step 1

 

 

 

 

 

Step 3: Configure Request Body

Use the following JSON payload:

{
  "model": "openai/gpt-4",
  "messages": [
    {
      "role": "system",
      "content": "You are an SAP CPI support expert."
    },
    {
      "role": "user",
      "content": "Analyze this SAP CPI error and provide Error, Cause, and Resolution: ${property.CamelExceptionCaught}"
    }
  ],
  "temperature": 0.3,
  "max_tokens": 400
}

 

 

Understanding the Roles

system Role

Defines AI behavior.

Example:

{
  "role": "system",
  "content": "You are an SAP CPI support expert."
}

This helps the model provide SAP-specific troubleshooting responses.

user Role

Contains the actual exception text from CPI.

Example:

{
  "role": "user",
  "content": "Analyze this SAP CPI error..."
}

 

Step 4: Capture Exception in Exception Subprocess

Inside the Exception Subprocess:

  • Add Content Modifier
  • Store exception into property:

                    ${exception.message} or ${property.CamelExceptionCaught}

  This property is then passed to the AI Adapter.

Step 5: Return AI Response to Sender

After AI Adapter execution:

  • Use Content Modifier or Groovy Script
  • Extract AI response
  • Return formatted response to sender

Example output:

Error:
Invalid endpoint configuration

Cause:
The HTTP receiver adapter contains an incorrect URL path.

Resolution:
Verify the endpoint path and ensure the API resource URL is valid.

 

 

 

 

Sample Use Cases

This approach can be used for:

    • HTTP adapter failures
    • SOAP faults
    • OData authentication errors
    • Mapping exceptions
    • XML parsing issues
    • API throttling analysis
    • Runtime deployment failures

 

Benefits of AI-Based Error Handling

Faster Troubleshooting

Developers receive instant suggestions without manually checking logs.

Reduced Support Effort

Support teams can automate first-level analysis.

Improved Developer Productivity

AI provides contextual recommendations and possible fixes.

Better Monitoring Experience

Instead of cryptic stack traces, users receive readable explanations.

entry.title: Why SAP Document AI Matters More Than Most Enterprises Think
entry.content:
Introduction

When everyone gets excited about the same topics, conversations will naturally center around them. Eventually, that excitement will catch on, and you’ll find yourself on the same wavelength as everyone else. Currently, as businesses focus on enterprise AI projects, most discussions revolve around similar themes: copilot, agents, and large language models. However, despite significant investments in AI, many businesses still face a common operational obstacle: documents.

Regardless of the industry, invoices, purchase orders, supplier forms, shipping documents, contracts, and compliance documents flow between departments daily. Each requires some degree of manual verification or validation and often involves multiple systems or workflows. This is where SAP Document AI comes in.

SAP Document AI transforms unstructured business documents into structured operational data, helping businesses modernize their operations. Instead of just digitizing your documents, you can use Document AI to make them useful and functional within your business. Even with advancements in intelligent front-end experiences, the foundational infrastructure supporting these solutions still relies on manual verification, various data extraction methods, and disconnected workflows. Document intelligence within SAP environments has matured, and companies now view it as a key automation initiative rather than a secondary option.

Our Experience

Adopting Document AI can reduce the need for labor to support business functions, leading to more efficient solutions. SAP Document AI addresses a common challenge in enterprise environments: extracting, understanding, and organizing information from business documents. In many companies, important operational data arrives in formats like PDFs, scanned files, emails, invoices, and forms, which ERP systems struggle to process without manual help.

SAP Document AI lowers this friction by using AI to identify document types, extract relevant fields, classify information, and prepare that data for business workflows. This means organizations can automate many document-heavy processes across finance, procurement, supply chain, HR, and other operational areas, rather than having employees manually review documents and transfer information between systems. The value of using Document AI goes beyond faster document processing; it empowers operational workflows to operate with greater speed, consistency, and scalability. As enterprises manage more operational data, minimizing manual document reliance becomes essential for maintaining efficiency.

We had the opportunity to see this first-hand. In this scenario, the company was operating in the oil and gas industry, where customer representatives and field engineers constantly generate operational documents while working across remote locations. Every service ticket, carrier document, export filing, client approval, invoice, or credit memo needed to be attached to the correct Sales Order inside SAP for compliance and operational traceability purposes. The challenge was that these documents arrived through emails in different languages, layouts, and formats, often with little context beyond a subject line. As a result, employees had to manually open every file, identify what type of document it was, locate the Sales Order reference somewhere inside the content, rename the file according to internal naming conventions, and upload it into SAP. At a scale of more than 8,000 documents per month, this translated into over 1,000 hours of manual effort every month, distributed across teams in multiple regions.

The organization already had partial automation in place through legacy capture tools and batch jobs, but the process still depended heavily on human intervention before the automation could even begin. A single typo in a file name or an incorrectly classified document could silently break the workflow and prevent the document from being linked correctly inside SAP. By implementing SAP Document AI on SAP BTP, the process shifted from manual interpretation to intelligent document processing. Incoming documents were automatically picked up through SAP Integration Suite, classified using SAP Document AI, and processed to extract key operational data such as Sales Order numbers, vendor details, amounts, and document dates. The extracted information was then validated and routed directly into SAP workflows, significantly reducing manual effort while improving scalability, consistency, and operational visibility across the entire process.

Why This Matters Beyond Document Processing

What made this project valuable was not just the automation itself. I would say it was rather the impact it had on daily operations. Once documents could be processed and validated in a more consistent way, teams spent far less time trying to interpret files manually or fixing small mistakes that slowed everything down. Workflows became easier to manage across different regions, and the process no longer depended so heavily on people knowing specific naming conventions or internal steps that were never formally documented.

Over time, we also realized that SAP Document AI was doing much more than helping with document processing. It became part of the operational structure supporting the entire workflow. As companies continue investing in SAP Business AI and intelligent workflows, having reliable operational data becomes increasingly important. If documents are still disconnected from the systems around them, scaling automation becomes much harder than expected.

Conclusion

As we said at the beginning, a lot of the conversation around enterprise AI focuses on what people can immediately see: copilots, AI agents, chat interfaces, and new user experiences. At the same time, many operational problems still happen behind the scenes, especially in processes where important business information is buried inside emails, PDFs, scanned files, and manual workflows.

Our experience with SAP Document AI showed us that document intelligence is no longer just a secondary automation tool. It can play an important role in helping businesses create cleaner and more scalable operations. When documents become structured operational data instead of isolated files, workflows move faster, teams spend less time on repetitive tasks, and enterprise systems become easier to scale over time.
